narrative: "If a herald or an ambassador carry a false message from our city to any other, or bring back a false message from the city to which he is sent, or be proved to have brought back, whether from friends or enemies, in his capacity of herald or ambassador, what they have never said, let him be indicted for having violated, contrary to the law, the commands and duties imposed upon him by Hermes and Zeus, and let there be a penalty fixed, which he shall suffer or pay if he be convicted."
refashioned: If any diplomatic envoy or official messenger deliberately transmits a false communication from our city to another, or brings back a fabricated report from the city to which he was sent, or is proven to have conveyed -- whether from allies or adversaries -- statements they never actually made, then he must be prosecuted for violating the sacred duties of his office, duties consecrated by the gods of communication and authority. A specific penalty shall be established, which he must suffer or pay upon conviction.
elegant: Any diplomat or envoy who falsifies messages -- whether outgoing or incoming, whether dealing with allies or enemies -- must be prosecuted for violating his sacred office. A fixed penalty shall apply upon conviction.
summary: Heralds and ambassadors who carry false messages, fabricate reports, or misrepresent what foreign parties have said must be prosecuted and penalized. The office of diplomatic communication is held sacred.
distilled: Diplomats who falsify messages betray a sacred trust and must face prosecution.
notes: The Athenian Stranger legislates on diplomatic integrity. The invocation of Hermes (god of messengers) and Zeus (god of oaths and authority) frames diplomacy as a sacred duty, not merely a political function. This reflects Plato's consistent view that political offices carry divine sanction -- compare Republic 389b-d, where lying is restricted to rulers acting for the city's benefit. The Laws takes a stricter position: even state representatives cannot distort truth in their official capacity.

narrative:
"Theft is a mean, and robbery a shameless thing; and none of the sons of Zeus delight in fraud and violence, or ever practised, either. Wherefore let no one be deluded by poets or mythologers into a mistaken belief of such things, nor let him suppose, when he thieves or is guilty of violence, that he is doing nothing base, but only what the Gods themselves do. For such tales are untrue and improbable; and he who steals or robs contrary to the law, is never either a God or the son of a God; of this the legislator ought to be better informed than all the poets put together."

"Happy is he and may he be forever happy, who is persuaded and listens to our words; but he who disobeys shall have to contend against the following law: If a man steal anything belonging to the public, whether that which he steals be much or little, he shall have the same punishment. For he who steals a little steals with the same wish as he who steals much, but with less power, and he who takes up a greater amount, not having deposited it, is wholly unjust. Wherefore the law is not disposed to inflict a less penalty on the one than on the other because his theft is less, but on the ground that the thief may possibly be in one case still curable, and may in another case be incurable. If any one convict in a court of law a stranger or a slave of a theft of public property, let the court determine what punishment he shall suffer, or what penalty he shall pay, bearing in mind that he is probably not incurable. But the citizen who has been brought up as our citizens will have been, if he be found guilty of robbing his country by fraud or violence, whether he be caught in the act or not, shall be punished with death; for he is incurable."
refashioned:
Theft is contemptible, and robbery is shameless. None of the gods take pleasure in fraud and violence, nor have they ever practiced such things. Therefore, let no one be misled by poets or storytellers into believing otherwise, nor imagine that in stealing or committing violence he is merely doing what the gods themselves do. Such stories are false and absurd. Anyone who steals or robs in violation of the law is neither a god nor descended from gods -- and the legislator should understand this better than all the poets combined.

Blessed is the person who accepts and follows these words. But whoever disobeys must face this law: if someone steals public property, whether the amount is large or small, the punishment shall be the same. The petty thief steals with the same corrupt intention as the grand thief -- he simply has less opportunity. And whoever appropriates a larger sum that was not rightfully his is wholly unjust. The law does not impose a lighter sentence because the theft was smaller. The only relevant distinction is whether the thief can still be reformed or is beyond redemption.

If a foreigner or slave is convicted of stealing public property, the court shall determine an appropriate punishment, keeping in mind that such a person is probably still capable of reform. But a citizen -- someone raised with all the advantages of our education -- who is found guilty of defrauding or robbing the state, whether caught in the act or not, shall be put to death. Such a person is beyond cure.
elegant:
Theft is contemptible, robbery shameless -- and no god endorses either. Let no one cite mythology to justify crime. The legislator knows better than all poets combined.

The law: all theft of public property receives equal punishment regardless of amount, because the criminal intent is identical -- only the opportunity differs. The sole distinction that matters is whether the thief can be reformed. Foreigners and slaves convicted of public theft may receive varied sentences, since reform remains possible. But a citizen raised under our laws who steals from the state -- by fraud or force, caught or uncaught -- shall die. He is incurable.
summary: Plato attacks the mythological justification for theft, insisting that no god endorses fraud or violence. Public theft receives equal punishment regardless of amount, since the criminal intent is the same. The critical distinction is reformability: foreigners and slaves may be rehabilitated, but a properly educated citizen who steals from the state is deemed incurable and must be executed.
distilled: All public theft is equally criminal in intent; the only question is whether the thief can be reformed, and a citizen who robs the state cannot be.
notes: The Athenian Stranger continues legislating. The attack on poets justifying theft through divine myths echoes Republic 377d-378e, where Plato censors stories of gods behaving immorally. The principle that criminal intent matters more than the amount stolen is remarkably modern. The harsh distinction between citizens and non-citizens reflects Plato's view that education creates greater moral obligation -- a citizen who steals despite proper upbringing reveals irredeemable corruption. Compare Republic 409e-410a on the incurable soul.

narrative:
"Now for expeditions of war much consideration and many laws are required; the great principle of all is that no one of either sex should be without a commander; nor should the mind of any one be accustomed to do anything, either in jest or earnest, of his own motion, but in war and in peace he should look to and follow his leader, even in the least things being under his guidance; for example, he should stand or move, or exercise, or wash, or take his meals, or get up in the night to keep guard and deliver messages when he is bidden; and in the hour of danger he should not pursue and not retreat except by order of his superior; and in a word, not teach the soul or accustom her to know or understand how to do anything apart from others."

"Of all soldiers the life should be always and in all things as far as possible in common and together; there neither is nor ever will be a higher, or better, or more scientific principle than this for the attainment of salvation and victory in war. And we ought in time of peace from youth upwards to practise this habit of commanding others, and of being commanded by others; anarchy should have no place in the life of man or of the beasts who are subject to man. I may add that all dances ought to be performed with a view to military excellence; and agility and ease should be cultivated for the same object, and also endurance of the want of meats and drinks, and of winter cold and summer heat, and of hard couches; and, above all, care should be taken not to destroy the peculiar qualities of the head and the feet by surrounding them with extraneous coverings, and so hindering their natural growth of hair and soles."
refashioned:
Military campaigns require extensive deliberation and many laws. The overriding principle is this: no person, male or female, should ever be without a commander. No one's mind should grow accustomed to acting independently, whether in serious matters or trivial ones. In war and in peace alike, every person should look to a leader and follow orders, even in the smallest things -- when to stand, when to move, when to exercise, wash, eat, or rise in the night for guard duty and message-carrying. In battle, no one should pursue or retreat except on a superior's command. In short, the soul should never learn or practice doing anything independently of the group.

For soldiers above all, life should be lived in common as much as possible. There is no higher, better, or more scientifically sound principle for achieving survival and victory in war than collective discipline. This habit of commanding and being commanded should be practiced from youth onward during peacetime as well. Anarchy has no place in human life, nor even in the management of animals.

Furthermore, all dances should be designed to develop military fitness. Agility and grace should be cultivated for the same purpose, along with the ability to endure hunger, thirst, cold, heat, and uncomfortable sleeping conditions. Above all, the head and feet should not be weakened by unnecessary coverings that prevent the natural toughening of hair and soles.
elegant:
The supreme military principle: no one should ever be without a commander. The mind must never grow accustomed to independent action. In war and peace, follow orders in everything -- movement, meals, guard duty, pursuit, retreat. The soul should not learn to act apart from the group.

Collective living is the highest principle for military success. Practice both commanding and obeying from youth. Anarchy belongs nowhere in human life. All physical training -- dance, endurance of hunger, cold, heat, discomfort -- should serve military readiness. Avoid softening the body with unnecessary protections.
summary: The supreme principle of military organization is that no one should ever act without a commander's direction. Soldiers should live communally, and the habit of commanding and obeying should be practiced from youth in peacetime. All physical training, including dance, should serve military fitness and hardiness.
distilled: The foundation of military excellence is absolute discipline under command, collective living, and physical hardening practiced from youth.
notes: The Athenian Stranger legislates on military organization. The extreme emphasis on collective discipline and the abolition of independent action echoes Republic 416d-417b (communal life of guardians) but goes further -- extending military discipline to all of peacetime life. The insistence that no one should act independently, even in jest, represents Plato's most authoritarian statement on social organization. The detail about bare heads and feet reflects Spartan training practices that Plato admired. Compare Sparta's agoge system and the militarized education in Republic 537a-b.

narrative:
"For these are the extremities, and of all the parts of the body, whether they are preserved or not is of the greatest consequence; the one is the servant of the whole body, and the other the master, in whom all the ruling senses are by nature set. Let the young man imagine that he hears in what has preceded the praises of the military life; the law shall be as follows:"

"He shall serve in war who is on the roll or appointed to some special service, and if any one is absent from cowardice, and without the leave of the generals, he shall be indicted before the military commanders for failure of service when the army comes home; and the soldiers shall be his judges; the heavy armed, and the cavalry, and the other arms of the service shall form separate courts; and they shall bring the heavy-armed before the heavy-armed, and the horsemen before the horsemen, and the others in like manner before their peers; and he who is found guilty shall never be allowed to compete for any prize of valour, or indict another for not serving on an expedition, or be an accuser at all in any military matters."

"Moreover, the court shall further determine what punishment he shall suffer, or what penalty he shall pay. When the suits for failure of service are completed, the leaders of the several kinds of troops shall again hold an assembly, and they shall adjudge the prizes of valour; and he who likes shall give judgment in his own branch of the service, saying nothing about any former expedition, nor producing any proof or witnesses to confirm his statement, but speaking only of the present occasion. The crown of victory shall be an olive wreath which the victor shall offer up at the temple of any war-god whom he likes, adding an inscription for a testimony to last during life, that such an one has received the first, the second, or third prize. If any one goes on an expedition, and returns home before the appointed time, when the generals have not withdrawn the army, he shall be indicted for desertion before the same persons who took cognisance of failure of service, and if he be found guilty, the same punishment shall be inflicted on him."
refashioned:
The feet and head are the body's extremities, and their condition matters most of all. The feet serve the whole body, while the head is its master, housing all the governing senses. Let the young man take what has been said as praise of the military life. Now the law:

Every person on the military rolls or assigned to special duty must serve when called. Anyone who is absent out of cowardice, without leave from the generals, shall be prosecuted for failure of service when the army returns home. His fellow soldiers shall judge him -- infantry judged by infantry, cavalry by cavalry, each branch trying its own. Anyone found guilty shall be permanently barred from competing for military honors, from accusing others of failing to serve, and from bringing any charges in military matters.

The court shall also determine additional punishment or fines. After these trials are concluded, the commanders of each branch shall convene assemblies to award prizes for valor. Each soldier may speak only about the current campaign in his own branch of service -- no references to past expeditions, no outside proof or witnesses. The prize shall be an olive wreath, which the winner shall dedicate at the temple of whichever war-god he chooses, with an inscription recording whether he won first, second, or third place -- a testimony lasting his lifetime.

Anyone who leaves a campaign before the appointed time, without the army having been formally withdrawn by the generals, shall be prosecuted for desertion before the same courts that handle failure of service, and shall receive the same punishment if convicted.
elegant:
The head and feet matter most -- one houses the senses that rule, the other serves the whole body. Now the military law:

Every enrolled person must serve. Cowardly absence without leave means prosecution by one's own branch of service upon return. The guilty are permanently barred from military honors and from bringing military accusations. Additional penalties are set by the court.

After these trials, valor prizes are awarded by branch. Soldiers speak only of the current campaign -- no past references, no outside witnesses. Winners receive olive wreaths dedicated at a war-god's temple with a lasting inscription. Desertion during an active campaign carries the same penalties as failure to serve.
summary: Military service is compulsory for all enrolled citizens, with cowardly absence judged by one's peers in the same branch of service. The guilty lose all military standing. Valor prizes are awarded strictly for the current campaign, and desertion carries the same penalties as failure to serve.
distilled: Cowardly absence from military duty is judged by one's peers and results in permanent disgrace, while valor is rewarded with olive wreaths and temple inscriptions.
notes: The Athenian Stranger establishes military courts organized by branch of service -- a peer-judgment system. The principle that only fellow soldiers can judge military conduct reflects a practical wisdom about expertise. The prohibition against using past campaigns as evidence for valor awards emphasizes present merit over accumulated reputation. The permanent stripping of military standing from cowards -- including the right to accuse others -- creates a comprehensive civic death in military affairs. Compare Republic 468a-e on honors for military valor.

narrative:
"Now every man who is engaged in any suit ought to be very careful of bringing false witness against any one, either intentionally or unintentionally, if he can help; for justice is truly said to be an honourable maiden, and falsehood is naturally repugnant to honour and justice. A witness ought to be very careful not to sin against justice, as for example in what relates to the throwing away of arms -- he must distinguish the throwing them away when necessary, and not make that a reproach, or bring an action against some innocent person on that account."

"To make the distinction may be difficult; but still the law must attempt to define the different kinds in some way. Let me endeavour to explain my meaning by an ancient tale: If Patroclus had been brought to the tent still alive but without his arms (and this has happened to innumerable persons), the original arms, which the poet says were presented to Peleus by the Gods as a nuptial gift when he married Thetis, remaining in the hands of Hector, then the base spirits of that day might have reproached the son of Menoetius with having cast away his arms."

"Again, there is the case of those who have been thrown down precipices and lost their arms; and of those who at sea, and in stormy places, have been suddenly overwhelmed by floods of water; and there are numberless things of this kind which one might adduce by way of extenuation, and with the view of justifying a misfortune which is easily misrepresented. We must, therefore, endeavour to divide to the best of our power the greater and more serious evil from the lesser. And a distinction may be drawn in the use of terms of reproach. A man does not always deserve to be called the thrower away of his shield; he may be only the loser of his arms."
refashioned:
Anyone involved in a legal proceeding must be extremely careful not to bear false witness -- intentionally or unintentionally. Justice is rightly called an honorable maiden, and falsehood is naturally offensive to both honor and justice. A witness must be particularly cautious not to sin against justice in matters like the loss of arms in battle. He must distinguish between losing arms through necessity and deliberately abandoning them, and must not bring accusations against innocent people who lost their weapons through no fault of their own.

Making this distinction may be difficult, but the law must try to define the different cases. Consider an ancient example: if Patroclus had been carried back to his tent alive but stripped of his armor -- as has happened to countless soldiers -- with the divine arms that the gods gave to Peleus at his wedding to Thetis now in Hector's hands, the petty-minded men of that era might have accused him of throwing away his weapons.

Or consider those who have fallen from cliffs and lost their arms, or those overwhelmed by sudden floods at sea or in storms. There are countless such circumstances that could explain the loss of weapons -- misfortunes easily misrepresented as cowardice. We must therefore do our best to distinguish the serious moral failure from the lesser misfortune. The very language of reproach should reflect this distinction: a man does not always deserve to be called one who "threw away" his shield. He may simply be one who "lost" his arms.
elegant:
No one should bear false witness in legal proceedings. Justice demands careful distinction, especially in military matters like the loss of arms. Losing weapons through necessity is entirely different from abandoning them through cowardice.

Consider Patroclus: had he survived but lost his divine armor to Hector, petty minds would have called him a coward. Consider soldiers swept off cliffs or overwhelmed by floods. Misfortune is easily misrepresented as moral failure.

The law must distinguish the serious evil from the lesser one. Even our language should reflect this: there is a difference between "throwing away" a shield and "losing" one's arms.
summary: Witnesses must be careful not to bring false accusations, especially in military matters. The law must distinguish between soldiers who lose their arms through misfortune and those who abandon them through cowardice. Even the language of reproach should reflect this difference -- losing one's shield is not the same as throwing it away.
distilled: Justice requires distinguishing between losing one's arms through misfortune and abandoning them through cowardice, and even our language of blame should reflect this difference.
notes: The Athenian Stranger uses Homer's Iliad to illustrate a legal principle. The Patroclus example (Iliad 16) is striking -- Plato, usually hostile to Homer, here uses epic narrative constructively to show how circumstances can be misread. The linguistic distinction between 'throwing away' (rhipsaspis) and 'losing' arms was a real concern in Greek military law; Archilochus famously boasted of throwing away his shield. This passage shows Plato's sensitivity to how language shapes moral judgment -- compare Cratylus on the power of names, and Statesman 291a-b on distinguishing similar-seeming acts.

narrative:
"For there is a great or rather absolute difference between him who is deprived of his arms by a sufficient force, and him who voluntarily lets his shield go. Let the law then be as follows: If a person having arms is overtaken by the enemy and does not turn round and defend himself, but lets them go voluntarily or throws them away, choosing a base life and a swift escape rather than a courageous and noble and blessed death -- in such a case of the throwing away of arms let justice be done, but the judge need take no note of the case just now mentioned; for the bad man ought always to be punished, in the hope that he may be improved, but not the unfortunate, for there is no advantage in that."

"And what shall be the punishment suited to him who has thrown away his weapons of defence? Tradition says that Caeneus, the Thessalian, was changed by a God from a woman into a man; but the converse miracle cannot now be wrought, or no punishment would be more proper than that the man who throws away his shield should be changed into a woman."
refashioned:
There is a vast -- indeed absolute -- difference between the soldier who loses his arms to overwhelming force and the one who voluntarily abandons his shield. The law shall therefore be as follows:

If a man bearing arms is overtaken by the enemy and, rather than turning to defend himself, voluntarily releases or throws away his weapons -- choosing a shameful survival and swift escape over a courageous, noble, and blessed death -- then he shall be punished for abandoning his arms. But the judge should take no notice of the case described earlier, where arms were lost through misfortune. The wicked man should always be punished, in the hope that punishment may improve him. But there is no point in punishing the merely unfortunate.

As for what punishment fits the man who throws away his weapons of defense: tradition tells us that Caeneus the Thessalian was transformed by a god from a woman into a man. If the reverse miracle were still possible, no punishment would be more appropriate than transforming the man who abandons his shield into a woman.
elegant:
There is an absolute difference between losing arms to superior force and voluntarily abandoning them. The law: anyone who throws away his weapons, choosing shameful escape over honorable death, shall be punished. But those who lost arms through misfortune shall not be -- punishing the unfortunate serves no purpose.

The fitting punishment? Legend says Caeneus was transformed from woman to man by a god. If the reverse were possible, it would be the perfect sentence for the coward who throws away his shield.
summary: The law distinguishes absolutely between losing arms through misfortune and voluntarily abandoning them. Only voluntary abandonment is punished, since punishing the unfortunate serves no purpose. Plato humorously suggests that the ideal punishment for cowardice would be transformation into a woman, referencing the myth of Caeneus.
distilled: Only the soldier who voluntarily abandons his weapons deserves punishment; the merely unfortunate should not suffer for misfortune beyond their control.
notes: The Athenian Stranger legislates on military cowardice. The principle that punishment should aim at improvement, not retribution against misfortune, is a cornerstone of Plato's penology -- compare Laws 862d-863a and Protagoras 324a-c. The Caeneus myth (a woman transformed into a man by Poseidon) serves as dark humor, but reveals an embedded gender hierarchy: cowardice is coded as feminine, courage as masculine. This gendered framing of virtue appears throughout Greek thought and in Republic 451d-e, though there Plato is more progressive about women's capabilities.

narrative:
"This however is impossible, and therefore let us make a law as nearly like this as we can -- that he who loves his life too well shall be in no danger for the remainder of his days, but shall live for ever under the stigma of cowardice. And let the law be in the following terms:"

"When a man is found guilty of disgracefully throwing away his arms in war, no general or military officer shall allow him to serve as a soldier, or give him any place at all in the ranks of soldiers; and the officer who gives the coward any place, shall suffer a penalty which the public examiner shall exact of him; and if he be of the highest class, he shall pay a thousand drachmae; or if he be of the second class, five minae; or if he be of the third, three minae; or if he be of the fourth class, one mina."

"And he who is found guilty of cowardice, shall not only be dismissed from manly dangers, which is a disgrace appropriate to his nature, but he shall pay a thousand drachmae, if he be of the highest class, and five minae if he be of the second class, and three if he be of the third class, and a mina, like the preceding, if he be of the fourth class."
refashioned:
Since such a transformation is impossible, let us make a law as close to it as we can: the man who loves his life too much shall face no danger for the rest of his days, but shall live forever under the stigma of cowardice. The law shall be as follows:

When a man is convicted of disgracefully throwing away his arms in war, no general or military officer shall permit him to serve as a soldier or assign him any place in the ranks. Any officer who gives the coward a position shall be penalized by the public examiner -- one thousand drachmae for the highest property class, five minae for the second, three minae for the third, and one mina for the fourth.

The convicted coward himself shall not only be permanently dismissed from military service -- a disgrace fitting his nature, since he who craves safety above all shall have it -- but shall also pay a fine: one thousand drachmae for the highest class, five minae for the second, three for the third, and one mina for the fourth.
elegant:
Since transformation is impossible, the closest equivalent: the man who loves life too much shall be granted his wish -- permanent safety, but with permanent stigma.

The law: convicted cowards are banned from military service forever. Any officer who reinstates one pays a fine scaled by property class. The coward himself is dismissed from all military danger -- a disgrace suited to his nature -- and fined according to the same class scale.
summary: Convicted cowards are permanently banned from military service and fined according to their property class. Officers who reinstate them are also penalized. The ironic punishment grants the coward exactly what he wanted -- safety -- but pairs it with lasting disgrace.
distilled: The coward who throws away his arms gets what he wanted -- permanent safety -- but at the price of permanent disgrace and financial penalty.
notes: The Athenian Stranger establishes the formal penalty for military cowardice. The ironic structure of the punishment -- granting the coward the safety he chose, but making it shameful -- is characteristic of Plato's approach to justice as fitting the crime to the character. The graduated fines by property class reflect the Laws' four-class economic system (Laws 744b-745b). The penalty on officers who reinstate cowards creates accountability up the chain of command. Compare the dishonor of cowards in Republic 468a and the Spartan practice of shaming those who fled battle (atimia).

narrative:
"What regulations will be proper about examiners, seeing that some of our magistrates are elected by lot, and for a year, and some for a longer time and from selected persons? Of such magistrates, who will be a sufficient censor or examiner, if any of them, weighed down by the pressure of office or his own inability to support the dignity of his office, be guilty of any crooked practice?"

"It is by no means easy to find a magistrate who excels other magistrates in virtue, but still we must endeavour to discover some censor or examiner who is more than man. For the truth is, that there are many elements of dissolution in a state, as there are also in a ship, or in an animal; they all have their cords, and girders, and sinews -- one nature diffused in many places, and called by many names; and the office of examiner is a most important element in the preservation and dissolution of states."
refashioned:
What regulations should govern the examiners of magistrates? Some of our officials are chosen by lot and serve for a year, while others are elected from distinguished candidates and serve longer terms. Given this variety, who can serve as an adequate censor or examiner -- especially when magistrates may be corrupted by the pressures of office or may prove unequal to its demands?

It is extremely difficult to find someone who surpasses other officials in virtue, yet we must try to discover an examiner of almost superhuman quality. The reality is that every state, like a ship or a living creature, contains many elements that can cause its dissolution. All complex systems have their cords, braces, and sinews -- a single binding nature distributed throughout and called by different names. The office of examiner is among the most critical of these binding elements. It can either preserve the state or accelerate its destruction.
elegant:
Who will examine the magistrates? Some officials are chosen by lot, others elected; some serve briefly, others for years. Finding someone who surpasses all other officials in virtue is enormously difficult, yet we must find examiners of near-superhuman quality.

Every state, like a ship or living body, has structural elements that hold it together -- and that cause its collapse when they fail. The office of examiner is among the most critical of these binding forces.
summary: The state needs examiners of magistrates, but finding individuals of sufficient virtue to judge other officials is extremely difficult. The office of examiner is compared to the vital structural elements in ships and living bodies -- a binding force that either preserves the state or causes its dissolution.
distilled: The office that examines magistrates is the structural sinew of the state, and finding someone virtuous enough to fill it is the hardest challenge in governance.
notes: The Athenian Stranger transitions to the critical topic of accountability mechanisms -- the euthyna (audit/examination) that magistrates undergo at the end of their terms. The organic metaphor of the state as a body with cords and sinews echoes Republic 462c-d (the state as unified body) and anticipates the more developed body-politic analogy later in Book 12. The admission that finding someone 'more than man' is needed reveals a fundamental tension in Plato's political thought -- the same problem of 'who guards the guardians' raised in Republic 403d. The Statesman 293a-e similarly struggles with finding rulers of adequate wisdom.

narrative:
"For if the examiners are better than the magistrates, and their duty is fulfilled justly and without blame, then the whole state and country flourishes and is happy; but if the examination of the magistrates is carried on in a wrong way, then, by the relaxation of that justice which is the uniting principle of all constitutions, every power in the state is rent asunder from every other; they no longer incline in the same direction, but fill the city with faction, and make many cities out of one, and soon bring all to destruction."

"Wherefore the examiners ought to be admirable in every sort of virtue. Let us invent a mode of creating them, which shall be as follows: Every year, after the summer solstice, the whole city shall meet in the common precincts of Helios and Apollo, and shall present to the God three men out of their own number in the manner following: Each citizen shall select, not himself, but some other citizen whom he deems in every way the best, and who is not less than fifty years of age. And out of the selected persons who have the greatest number of votes, they shall make a further selection until they reduce them to one-half, if they are an even number; but if they are not an even number, they shall subtract the one who has the smallest number of votes, and make them an even number, and then leave the half which have the greater number of votes."
refashioned:
If the examiners are superior in virtue to the magistrates, and they fulfill their duty justly and blamelessly, then the entire state flourishes. But if the examination of magistrates is conducted improperly, then justice -- the unifying principle of all constitutions -- is weakened, and every power in the state is torn away from every other. The parts no longer move in the same direction. The city fills with faction, one city becomes many, and destruction follows swiftly.

Therefore the examiners must be admirable in every kind of virtue. Here is the method for selecting them: Every year, after the summer solstice, the entire citizenry shall gather in the sacred precincts of Helios and Apollo to present three men to the gods from among their number. The procedure is as follows: each citizen shall nominate not himself but another citizen whom he considers the best in every respect, provided that person is at least fifty years old. From those who receive the most votes, they shall make a further cut, reducing the number by half. If the total is odd, they shall first remove the candidate with the fewest votes to make the number even, and then retain the half with the greater number of votes.
elegant:
Good examiners mean a flourishing state. Bad examination means the collapse of justice -- the unifying principle of all constitutions. When justice relaxes, every power pulls in a different direction, faction fills the city, one city becomes many, and destruction follows.

The selection method: annually, after the summer solstice, all citizens gather at the precincts of Helios and Apollo. Each nominates another citizen (not himself) whom he considers the best, aged fifty or older. From the top vote-getters, the field is halved through successive rounds of elimination.
summary: Competent examiners are the difference between a flourishing state and one torn apart by faction. The selection process involves annual assembly of all citizens, who nominate others (not themselves) over fifty years of age, with successive rounds of elimination to narrow the field.
distilled: When the examination of magistrates fails, justice -- the binding force of all constitutions -- dissolves, and the state fractures into warring factions.
notes: The Athenian Stranger describes the selection process for examiners (euthynoi). The principle that justice is the 'uniting principle of all constitutions' echoes Republic 351d-352a, where justice is the bond that holds any group together. The specific warning that bad examination 'makes many cities out of one' parallels Republic 422e-423a on the fragmented city. The elaborate democratic selection process -- nomination of others, age requirements, successive elimination rounds -- shows the Laws' more practical approach compared to the Republic's philosopher-kings. The sacred setting (precincts of Helios and Apollo) sanctifies the political process.

narrative:
"And if two persons have an equal number of votes, and thus increase the number beyond one-half, they shall withdraw the younger of the two and do away with the excess; and then including all the rest they shall again vote, until there are left three having an unequal number of votes. But if all the three, or two out of the three, have equal votes, let them commit the election to good fate and fortune, and separate off by lot the first, and the second, and the third; these they shall crown with an olive wreath and give them the prize of excellence, at the same time proclaiming to all the world that the city of the Magnetes, by providence of the Gods, is again preserved, and presents to the Sun and to Apollo her three best men as first-fruits, to be a common offering to them, according to the ancient law, as long as their lives answer to the judgment formed of them."

"And these shall appoint in their first year twelve examiners, to continue until each has completed seventy-five years, to whom three shall afterwards be added yearly; and let these divide all the magistracies into twelve parts, and prove the holders of them by every sort of test to which a freeman may be subjected; and let them live while they hold office in the precinct of Helios and Apollo, in which they were chosen, and let each one form a judgment of some things individually, and of others in company with his colleagues; and let him place a writing in the agora about each magistracy, and what the magistrate ought to suffer or pay, according to the decision of the examiners."

"And if a magistrate does not admit that he has been justly judged, let him bring the examiners before the select judges, and if he be acquitted by their decision, let him, if he will, accuse the examiners themselves; if, however, he be convicted, and have been condemned to death by the examiners, let him die (and of course he can only die once) -- but any other penalties which admit of being doubled let him suffer twice over."
refashioned:
If two candidates are tied and this pushes the number above half, the younger of the two shall be removed to restore the correct count. Voting continues until three remain with unequal vote totals. But if all three, or two of the three, are tied, the matter shall be entrusted to fortune and decided by lot -- first place, second, and third. The three winners shall be crowned with olive wreaths and awarded the prize of excellence, while it is publicly proclaimed that the city of Magnesia, by the providence of the gods, is once again preserved and offers its three best citizens to the Sun and Apollo as first-fruits -- a sacred offering according to ancient custom, valid so long as their lives prove worthy of the judgment.

In their first year, these three shall appoint twelve examiners who serve until each reaches seventy-five years of age. Three new examiners are added each year thereafter. These examiners shall divide all magistracies into twelve categories and test the officeholders by every legitimate means applicable to free citizens. During their tenure, they shall reside in the sacred precinct of Helios and Apollo where they were chosen. Each examiner shall form judgments both independently and in consultation with colleagues, and shall post public reports in the marketplace detailing the findings for each office and whatever penalty the magistrate should suffer or pay.

If a magistrate disputes the examiners' verdict, he may bring the case before the select judges. If acquitted, he may then prosecute the examiners themselves. But if convicted, and sentenced to death by the examiners, he shall die -- he can only die once, after all. Any other penalty that admits of doubling shall be doubled.
elegant:
Ties are broken by removing the younger candidate; if the final three are tied, lots decide the ranking. The winners receive olive wreaths and are proclaimed as the city's offering to the Sun and Apollo -- valid so long as their conduct merits the honor.

These three appoint twelve examiners (serving until seventy-five, with three added yearly), who divide all offices into twelve categories, test every magistrate by legitimate means, reside in the sacred precinct, and post public findings in the marketplace.

Magistrates may appeal to the select judges. If acquitted, they may counter-prosecute the examiners. If convicted and sentenced to death, the sentence stands. Any other penalty is doubled.
summary: The three top examiners are chosen through iterative voting and honored as sacred offerings to the gods. They appoint twelve examiners who audit all magistrates, live in the sacred precinct, and publish their findings publicly. Magistrates may appeal, but if convicted a second time, penalties are doubled -- and death sentences stand.
distilled: The examiners form a sacred institution that publicly audits all magistrates, with appeals possible but double penalties for those who challenge their verdicts and lose.
notes: The Athenian Stranger completes the examiner selection process. The detail that examiners reside in the sacred precinct makes them quasi-priestly figures -- their authority is simultaneously political and religious. The appeal mechanism (magistrate to select judges, then possible counter-prosecution of examiners) creates a sophisticated system of checks. The dry humor about dying only once is a rare moment of levity in the Laws. The doubling of penalties for failed appeals discourages frivolous challenges. This entire institutional design represents Plato's mature attempt to solve the 'who guards the guardians' problem through procedural mechanisms rather than the Republic's reliance on philosophical virtue alone.

narrative:
"And now let us pass under review the examiners themselves; what will their examination be, and how conducted? During the life of these men, whom the whole state counts worthy of the rewards of virtue, they shall have the first seat at all public assemblies, and at all Hellenic sacrifices and sacred missions, and other public and holy ceremonies in which they share. The chiefs of each sacred mission shall be selected from them, and they only of all the citizens shall be adorned with a crown of laurel; they shall all be priests of Apollo and Helios; and one of them, who is judged first of the priests created in that year, shall be high priest; and they shall write up his name in each year to be a measure of time as long as the city lasts; and after their death they shall be laid out and carried to the grave and entombed in a manner different from the other citizens."

"They shall be decked in a robe all of white, and there shall be no crying or lamentation over them; but a chorus of fifteen maidens, and another of boys, shall stand around the bier on either side, hymning the praises of the departed priests in alternate responses, declaring their blessedness in song all day long; and at dawn a hundred of the youths who practise gymnastic and whom the relations of the departed shall choose, shall carry the bier to the sepulchre, the young men marching first, dressed in the garb of warriors -- the cavalry with their horses, the heavy-armed with their arms, and the others in like manner. And boys near the bier and in front of it shall sing their national hymn, and maidens shall follow behind, and with them the women who have passed the age of childbearing; next, although they are interdicted from other burials, let priests and priestesses follow, unless the Pythian oracle forbid them; for this burial is free from pollution."
refashioned:
Now let us consider the examiners themselves -- what review do they undergo, and how is it conducted? During their lifetimes, these men whom the entire state deems worthy of virtue's rewards shall have the seat of honor at all public assemblies, at all pan-Hellenic sacrifices and sacred delegations, and at every public and sacred ceremony they attend. The leaders of each sacred delegation shall be chosen from among them. They alone among all citizens shall wear a crown of laurel. They shall all serve as priests of Apollo and Helios, and the one judged first among the priests appointed that year shall be high priest. His name shall be recorded each year as a measure of time for as long as the city endures.

At death, they shall be laid out and buried in a manner distinct from all other citizens. They shall be dressed entirely in white, and there shall be no weeping or lamentation. Instead, a chorus of fifteen girls and another of boys shall stand on either side of the bier, singing the praises of the departed in alternating responses and declaring his blessedness in song throughout the day. At dawn, one hundred young athletes, chosen by the family of the deceased, shall carry the bier to the tomb. The young men shall march ahead in full military dress -- cavalry with horses, infantry in armor, and the other branches likewise. Boys shall walk near and in front of the bier singing the national hymn, with maidens following behind accompanied by women past childbearing age. Priests and priestesses, though normally forbidden to attend burials, shall join this procession unless the Pythian oracle forbids it, for this burial carries no pollution.
elegant:
The examiners themselves receive ongoing review. In life, they hold the highest ceremonial honors: first seats at assemblies, leadership of sacred delegations, laurel crowns, and priesthood of Apollo and Helios. The chief among each year's priests serves as high priest, his name recorded as a marker of time.

In death, they receive unique rites: white robes, no lamentation, but choruses of boys and girls singing praises in alternation all day. A procession of one hundred young athletes in military dress carries the bier at dawn, with national hymns, maidens, elder women, and priests following -- an exception to the rule barring clergy from funerals.
summary: Examiners receive the highest honors in life -- ceremonial precedence, laurel crowns, and priestly office. In death, they receive unique rites: white robes instead of mourning, choral praise instead of lamentation, and a grand military procession. Even priests may attend, though normally forbidden from funerals.
distilled: The examiners are honored as the state's highest spiritual authorities in life and given unique celebratory funeral rites in death.
notes: The Athenian Stranger describes the extraordinary honors for examiners. The practice of using the high priest's name as a chronological marker (eponymous dating) was standard in Athens with the archon eponymous. The funeral rites are deliberately anti-tragic -- joy replaces lamentation, white replaces mourning colors. This reflects Plato's long-standing critique of excessive mourning (Republic 387d-388e) and his view that death is not an evil for the virtuous. The exception for priests attending this burial (normally forbidden due to pollution concerns) elevates the examiners above ordinary mortals.

narrative:
"The place of burial shall be an oblong vaulted chamber underground, constructed of tufa, which will last for ever, having stone couches placed side by side. And here they will lay the blessed person, and cover the sepulchre with a circular mound of earth and plant a grove of trees around on every side but one; and on that side the sepulchre shall be allowed to extend for ever, and a new mound will not be required. Every year they shall have contests in music and gymnastics, and in horsemanship, in honour of the dead."

"These are the honours which shall be given to those who at the examination are found blameless; but if any of them, trusting to the scrutiny being over, should, after the judgment has been given, manifest the wickedness of human nature, let the law ordain that he who pleases shall indict him, and let the cause be tried in the following manner. In the first place, the court shall be composed of the guardians of the law, and to them the surviving examiners shall be added, as well as the court of select judges; and let the pursuer lay his indictment in this form -- he shall say that so-and-so is unworthy of the prize of virtue and of his office; and if the defendant be convicted let him be deprived of his office, and of the burial, and of the other honours given him. But if the prosecutor do not obtain the fifth part of the votes, let him, if he be of the first class, pay twelve minae, and eight if he be of the second class, and six if he be of the third class, and two minae if he be of the fourth class."
refashioned:
The burial place shall be an underground vaulted chamber of oblong shape, built from tufa stone that will last indefinitely, with stone couches placed side by side inside. Here they shall lay the blessed dead and cover the tomb with a circular earthen mound, planting a grove of trees on all sides except one. That open side shall allow the burial ground to expand as needed, so that new mounds are never necessary. Every year, contests in music, gymnastics, and horsemanship shall be held in their honor.

These are the honors given to examiners who are found blameless upon review. But if any of them, believing the scrutiny to be over, later reveals the worst of human nature, then any citizen who wishes may bring an indictment against him. The trial shall proceed as follows: the court shall consist of the guardians of the law, the surviving examiners, and the court of select judges. The prosecutor shall charge that the accused is unworthy of virtue's prize and of his office. If convicted, the examiner shall be stripped of his office, his special burial rights, and all honors previously granted. However, if the prosecutor fails to obtain even one-fifth of the votes, he shall pay a fine: twelve minae for the first property class, eight for the second, six for the third, and two minae for the fourth.
elegant:
The tomb: an underground vaulted chamber of lasting tufa, with stone couches, covered by an earthen mound and a grove of trees, expandable on one side. Annual contests in music, gymnastics, and horsemanship honor the dead.

But these honors apply only to the blameless. If an examiner later proves unworthy, any citizen may indict him before a joint court of law-guardians, surviving examiners, and select judges. Conviction means loss of office, burial rights, and all honors. But frivolous prosecution is deterred: a prosecutor who fails to win even one-fifth of the votes pays a steep fine scaled by property class.
summary: Examiners receive permanent honored burial in an expandable sacred grove with annual commemorative games. However, if an examiner later proves corrupt, any citizen may prosecute him before a combined court, and conviction strips all honors. Frivolous prosecution is deterred by heavy fines for prosecutors who fail to win even a fifth of the votes.
distilled: The honors of virtuous examiners extend beyond death into sacred burial and annual games, but corruption discovered later strips everything away.
notes: The Athenian Stranger completes the system of examiner accountability. The expandable burial ground -- open on one side -- is a practical detail showing the Laws' attention to long-term institutional planning. The one-fifth vote threshold for penalizing prosecutors mirrors Athenian law, where a prosecutor in certain public suits (graphe) who failed to win one-fifth of votes was fined 1000 drachmae. This mechanism balances accountability against vexatious litigation. The possibility that even proven virtue may conceal 'the wickedness of human nature' reflects Plato's sober view of human fallibility -- contrast with the Republic's more optimistic view of the philosopher-king's incorruptibility.

narrative: "The so-called decision of Rhadamanthus is worthy of all admiration. He knew that the men of his own time believed and had no doubt that there were Gods, which was a reasonable belief in those days, because most men were the sons of Gods, and according to tradition he was one himself. He appears to have thought that he ought to commit judgment to no man, but to the Gods only, and in this way suits were simply and speedily decided by him. For he made the two parties take an oath respecting the points in dispute, and so got rid of the matter speedily and safely."
refashioned: The celebrated method of Rhadamanthus deserves great admiration. He knew that the people of his era believed without question in the existence of the gods -- a reasonable belief in those times, since most men were considered descendants of gods, and Rhadamanthus himself was traditionally held to be one. He evidently concluded that judgment should be entrusted not to any human being but to the gods alone. Under his system, legal disputes were resolved simply and swiftly: he required both parties to swear an oath regarding the points in dispute, and the matter was settled quickly and reliably.
elegant: Rhadamanthus' judicial method was admirably simple: in an age when everyone genuinely believed in the gods, he required both parties to swear oaths about the disputed facts and let divine judgment handle the rest. Cases were resolved swiftly and reliably.
summary: Rhadamanthus resolved legal disputes by requiring both parties to swear oaths, effectively entrusting judgment to the gods. This worked in an age of genuine religious belief, when most people were considered descendants of the divine.
distilled: Rhadamanthus' oath-based justice worked because his age genuinely believed the gods would punish perjury.
notes: The Athenian Stranger introduces Rhadamanthus -- son of Zeus and Europa, mythological judge of the dead (see also Gorgias 523e-524a, where he judges souls in the underworld). This passage sets up a contrast between primitive oath-based justice and the rational legal procedures needed in a more skeptical age. The key insight is that legal institutions must match the society's actual beliefs -- a principle of institutional realism that distinguishes the Laws from the Republic's more idealistic approach. The praise of Rhadamanthus is genuine but preparatory to the argument that his methods are obsolete.

narrative:
"But now that a certain portion of mankind do not believe at all in the existence of the Gods, and others imagine that they have no care of us, and the opinion of most men, and of the worst men, is that in return for small sacrifice and a few flattering words they will be their accomplices in purloining large sums and save them from many terrible punishments, the way of Rhadamanthus is no longer suited to the needs of justice; for as the beliefs of men about the Gods are changed, the laws should also be changed."

"In the granting of suits a rational legislation ought to do away with the oaths of the parties on either side -- he who obtains leave to bring an action should write down the charges, but should not add an oath; and the defendant in like manner should give his denial to the magistrates in writing, and not swear; for it is a dreadful thing to know, when many lawsuits are going on in a state, that almost half the people who meet one another quite unconcernedly at the public meals and in other companies and relations of private life are perjured."

"Let the law, then, be as follows: A judge who is about to give judgment shall take an oath, and he who is choosing magistrates for the state shall either vote on oath or with a voting tablet which he brings from a temple; so too the judge of dances and of all music, and the superintendents and umpires of gymnastic and equestrian contests, and any matters in which, as far as men can judge, there is nothing to be gained by a false oath; but all cases in which a denial confirmed by an oath clearly results in a great advantage to the taker of the oath, shall be decided without the oath of the parties to the suit, and the presiding judges shall not permit either of them to use an oath for the sake of persuading, nor to call down curses on himself and his race, nor to use unseemly supplications or womanish laments."
refashioned:
But now a portion of humanity does not believe in the gods at all. Others believe the gods exist but take no interest in human affairs. And the majority -- the worst sort of people -- believe the gods can be bribed with small sacrifices and flattering words to become accomplices in fraud and to shield them from punishment. Under these conditions, Rhadamanthus' method is no longer adequate. When beliefs about the gods change, the laws must change accordingly.

Rational legislation should therefore eliminate party oaths in lawsuits. The plaintiff should file his charges in writing without an oath, and the defendant should submit his written denial to the magistrates without swearing. It is appalling to realize that in a state with many active lawsuits, nearly half the people one casually encounters at public meals and in private social life are perjurers.

The law shall be as follows: Judges shall take an oath before rendering verdicts. Those voting for magistrates shall either swear an oath or use a voting tablet brought from a temple. The same applies to judges of dance and music, and to supervisors and referees of athletic and equestrian competitions -- all cases where, as far as one can tell, a false oath offers no advantage. But in all cases where an oath-backed denial would provide clear material advantage, the parties' oaths shall be eliminated entirely. Presiding judges shall not permit either party to use oaths for persuasion, to call down curses on themselves and their families, or to resort to undignified appeals and excessive emotional displays.
elegant:
Now that some deny the gods entirely, others think the gods indifferent, and most believe the gods can be bribed, oath-based justice is obsolete. Changed beliefs require changed laws.

Rational legislation must eliminate party oaths in lawsuits. Both charges and defenses should be submitted in writing, without swearing. It is horrifying that in a litigious state, nearly half the citizenry are effectively perjurers.

The revised system: judges and voters for magistrates still swear oaths, as do officials judging competitions where false oaths offer no advantage. But wherever an oath-backed denial would provide material gain, party oaths are abolished. No emotional manipulation, no self-cursing, no theatrical displays in court.
summary: Since religious belief has declined and most people view the gods as bribable, oath-based justice no longer works. Rational legislation must eliminate party oaths in lawsuits to prevent mass perjury, while retaining oaths only for judges, voters, and officials in contexts where false swearing offers no advantage. Emotional manipulation in court is also banned.
distilled: When society no longer genuinely fears divine punishment for perjury, rational law must replace oath-based justice with written procedures.
notes: The Athenian Stranger's argument that legal institutions must adapt to changing religious beliefs is remarkably sociological. The three categories of religious failure -- atheism, deism (gods don't care), and transactional religion (gods can be bribed) -- correspond exactly to the three impieties analyzed in Laws Book 10 (885b-899d). The practical concern about mass perjury shows Plato at his most empirical. The elimination of party oaths anticipates modern legal systems that rely on evidence rather than sworn testimony of parties. Compare Republic 382a-c on the danger of lies, and the Euthyphro on the relationship between piety and justice.

narrative: "But they shall ever be teaching and learning what is just in auspicious words; and he who does otherwise shall be supposed to speak beside the point, and the judges shall again bring him back to the question at issue. On the other hand, strangers in their dealings with strangers shall as at present have power to give and receive oaths, for they will not often grow old in the city or leave a fry of young ones like themselves to be the sons and heirs of the land."
refashioned:
The parties in a lawsuit shall instead focus on teaching and learning what is just, using fitting and measured language. Anyone who departs from this standard shall be considered to be speaking off the point, and the judges shall redirect him to the matter at hand.

However, foreigners dealing with other foreigners may continue to give and receive oaths as they do now, since they are unlikely to grow old in the city or leave behind a generation of children to inherit the land.
elegant: Litigants should focus on demonstrating justice through measured argument. Those who stray from the point shall be redirected by judges. Foreigners dealing with foreigners may still use oaths, since they will not remain permanently or raise families in the city.
summary: In court, parties should focus on demonstrating justice through reasoned argument rather than oaths and emotional appeals. Foreigners may still use oaths in their dealings with each other, since they are transient residents without lasting ties to the city.
distilled: Courts should be forums for reasoned argument about justice, not theaters of emotional manipulation; only transient foreigners may still rely on oaths.
notes: The Athenian Stranger completes the reform of oath-based justice. The vision of courts as places of 'teaching and learning what is just' transforms litigation from adversarial combat into a quasi-educational process -- consistent with Plato's view that wrongdoing stems from ignorance (Protagoras 345d-e). The exception for foreigners is pragmatic: oaths still serve as a social bond for those without permanent stakes in the community. The concern about foreigners not 'leaving a fry of young ones' reveals the demographic anxiety underlying ancient citizenship restrictions.

narrative: "As to the initiation of private suits, let the manner of deciding causes between all citizens be the same as in cases in which any freeman is disobedient to the state in minor matters, of which the penalty is not stripes, imprisonment, or death. But as regards attendance at choruses or processions or other shows, and as regards public services, whether the celebration of sacrifice in peace, or the payment of contributions in war -- in all these cases, first comes the necessity of providing remedy for the loss; and by those who will not obey, there shall be security given to the officers whom the city and the law empower to exact the sum due; and if they forfeit their security, let the goods which they have pledged be sold, and the money given to the city; but if they ought to pay a larger sum, the several magistrates shall impose upon the disobedient a suitable penalty, and bring them before the court, until they are willing to do what they are ordered."
refashioned:
Regarding private lawsuits, the procedure for resolving disputes between citizens shall follow the same process used when a free person is disobedient to the state in minor matters -- those not warranting corporal punishment, imprisonment, or death.

However, when citizens fail to fulfill public obligations -- attendance at choruses, processions, or festivals, or public services such as religious celebrations in peacetime or financial contributions during war -- the first priority is to remedy the loss. Those who refuse to comply shall be required to post security with the officials whom the city and law authorize to collect what is owed. If they forfeit their security, the pledged goods shall be seized and sold, with the proceeds going to the city. If the amount owed exceeds the security, the relevant magistrates shall impose additional penalties on the disobedient and bring them before the court, continuing until they comply with their obligations.
elegant: Private lawsuits between citizens follow the same procedures as minor disobedience cases. But for failure to fulfill public obligations -- attendance at festivals, religious celebrations, or wartime contributions -- the loss must first be remedied. Non-compliers post security; if forfeited, their pledged goods are sold for the city. If the debt exceeds the security, magistrates impose further penalties and court appearances until compliance.
summary: Private lawsuits follow standard minor-offense procedures. Citizens who shirk public duties -- whether festival attendance, religious service, or wartime contributions -- must first make good the loss, posting security that can be forfeited, with escalating penalties until they comply.
distilled: Citizens who shirk public obligations face escalating enforcement until they comply, beginning with security deposits and escalating through court penalties.
notes: The Athenian Stranger legislates on civic obligation enforcement. The inclusion of festival attendance and chorus participation alongside wartime contributions reveals how deeply public religious and cultural life was interwoven with civic duty in Plato's vision. This is not merely about taxes -- it encompasses the full range of communal participation. The graduated enforcement mechanism (security, forfeiture, additional penalties, court) shows practical legal thinking. Compare Laws 764d-e on the superintendence of festivals.

narrative: "Now a state which makes money from the cultivation of the soil only, and has no foreign trade, must consider what it will do about the emigration of its own people to other countries, and the reception of strangers from elsewhere. About these matters the legislator has to consider, and he will begin by trying to persuade men as far as he can."
refashioned: A state that derives its wealth solely from agriculture and has no foreign trade must carefully consider two related questions: what to do about citizens who wish to travel to other countries, and how to handle the reception of foreigners. The legislator must address both matters, and he should begin by attempting persuasion rather than compulsion.
elegant: An agricultural state without foreign trade must decide its policy on citizen emigration and foreign reception. The legislator should begin with persuasion.
summary: An agrarian state without foreign trade must develop policies on both citizen travel abroad and the reception of foreigners, beginning with persuasion rather than coercion.
distilled: An isolated agricultural state must thoughtfully regulate both outward travel and inward immigration, starting with persuasion.
notes: The Athenian Stranger introduces the topic of foreign relations and travel policy. The emphasis on Magnesia as an agricultural state without trade connects to Laws 704a-705b (the city's distance from the sea to avoid commercial corruption). The principle of persuasion before compulsion is a recurring theme in the Laws (see the 'preludes' to laws, 719e-724b). This passage transitions into one of the Laws' most nuanced discussions of cultural exchange versus cultural preservation.

narrative:
"The intercourse of cities with one another is apt to create a confusion of manners; strangers are always suggesting novelties to strangers. When states are well governed by good laws the mixture causes the greatest possible injury; but seeing that most cities are the reverse of well-ordered, the confusion which arises in them from the reception of strangers, and from the citizens themselves rushing off into other cities, when any one either young or old desires to travel anywhere abroad at whatever time, is of no consequence."

"On the other hand, the refusal of states to receive others, and for their own citizens never to go to other places, is an utter impossibility, and to the rest of the world is likely to appear ruthless and uncivilized; it is a practice adopted by people who use harsh words, such as xenelasia or banishment of strangers, and who have harsh and morose ways, as men think."

"And to be thought or not to be thought well of by the rest of the world is no light matter; for the many are not so far wrong in their judgment of who are bad and who are good, as they are removed from the nature of virtue in themselves. Even bad men have a divine instinct which guesses rightly, and very many who are utterly depraved form correct notions and judgments of the differences between the good and bad. And the generality of cities are quite right in exhorting us to value a good reputation in the world, for there is no truth greater and more important than this -- that he who is really good (I am speaking of the man who would be perfect) seeks for reputation with, but not without, the reality of goodness. And our Cretan colony ought also to acquire the fairest and noblest reputation for virtue from other men; and there is every reason to expect that, if the reality answers to the idea, she will be one of the few well-ordered cities which the sun and the other Gods behold."
refashioned:
Contact between cities tends to create a confusion of customs; foreigners are always introducing novelties to one another. For well-governed states with good laws, this mixing causes the greatest possible harm. But since most cities are far from well-ordered, the chaos created by receiving foreigners or by their own citizens traveling freely abroad -- young and old, at any time, for any reason -- hardly makes things worse.

On the other hand, completely refusing to receive foreigners or forbidding citizens from ever traveling is both practically impossible and likely to appear brutal and uncivilized to the rest of the world. That approach -- known by the harsh name xenelasia, the banishment of strangers -- is associated with people of harsh and unsociable character.

Reputation in the wider world is no trivial matter. The general public is not as far wrong in judging who is good and who is bad as it is far removed from virtue in its own conduct. Even wicked people have a kind of divine instinct for recognizing goodness, and many thoroughly depraved individuals form quite accurate judgments about the difference between good and bad character. Most cities are right to encourage valuing one's reputation, for there is no greater truth than this: the truly good person -- one who aspires to perfection -- seeks reputation that accompanies real goodness, not reputation without it.

Our Cretan colony should likewise earn the noblest reputation for virtue among other peoples. And there is every reason to expect that if the reality matches the ideal, it will be among the few well-ordered cities that the sun and the gods look upon with favor.
elegant:
Contact between cities creates cultural confusion -- harmful to well-governed states, though irrelevant to poorly governed ones. Yet total isolation is impossible and appears uncivilized. Xenelasia -- banishing foreigners -- earns only contempt.

Reputation matters more than we might think. Even morally deficient people possess a reliable instinct for recognizing genuine virtue in others. The deepest truth about reputation: the truly good person seeks renown accompanied by real goodness, not mere appearances. Our colony should earn the noblest reputation through genuine virtue, becoming one of the few well-governed cities the gods observe with approval.
summary: Cultural contact between cities can corrupt good governance but cannot be entirely prevented without appearing barbaric. International reputation matters because even morally flawed people can accurately judge virtue in others. The colony should pursue genuine goodness that naturally produces a noble reputation, rather than reputation without substance.
distilled: Reputation matters because even the morally imperfect can recognize genuine virtue, so the truly good state seeks fame accompanied by real goodness, not appearances alone.
notes: The Athenian Stranger presents a sophisticated analysis of cultural exchange. The reference to xenelasia directly names Sparta's practice of periodically expelling foreigners -- a rare explicit critique of Spartan policy in the Laws. The epistemological claim that even bad people can recognize goodness through 'divine instinct' is striking and somewhat in tension with the Republic's emphasis on knowledge as the basis of moral judgment. The formulation that one should seek 'reputation with, but not without, the reality of goodness' elegantly resolves the tension between Socratic indifference to reputation (Apology 28b) and practical political necessity. Compare Gorgias 527a-b on true versus false reputation.

narrative:
"Wherefore, in the matter of journeys to other countries and the reception of strangers, we enact as follows: In the first place, let no one be allowed to go anywhere at all into a foreign country who is less than forty years of age; and no one shall go in a private capacity, but only in some public one, as a herald, or on an embassy, or on a sacred mission. Going abroad on an expedition or in war is not to be included among travels of the class authorized by the state."

"To Apollo at Delphi and to Zeus at Olympia and to Nemea and to the Isthmus, citizens should be sent to take part in the sacrifices and games there dedicated to the Gods; and they should send as many as possible, and the best and fairest that can be found, and they will make the city renowned at holy meetings in time of peace, procuring a glory which shall be the converse of that which is gained in war; and when they come home they shall teach the young that the institutions of other states are inferior to their own."

"And they shall send spectators of another sort, if they have the consent of the guardians, being such citizens as desire to look a little more at leisure at the doings of other men; and these no law shall hinder. For a city which has no experience of good and bad men or intercourse with them, can never be thoroughly and perfectly civilized, nor, again, can the citizens of a city properly observe the laws by habit only, and without an intelligent understanding of them."

"And there always are in the world a few inspired men whose acquaintance is beyond price, and who spring up quite as much in ill-ordered as in well-ordered cities. These are they whom the citizens of a well-ordered city should be ever seeking out, going forth over sea and over land to find him who is incorruptible -- that he may establish more firmly institutions in his own state which are good already, and amend what is deficient; for without this examination and enquiry a city will never continue perfect any more than if the examination is ill-conducted."

Cleinias: "How can we have an examination and also a good one?"
refashioned:
Therefore, regarding foreign travel and the reception of strangers, we enact the following: First, no citizen under forty years of age shall travel abroad under any circumstances. No one shall travel in a private capacity -- only in a public role such as herald, ambassador, or member of a sacred delegation. Military campaigns abroad are a separate category, not covered by these travel regulations.

Citizens should be sent to the festivals at Delphi for Apollo, Olympia for Zeus, Nemea, and the Isthmus to participate in the sacrifices and games dedicated to the gods. As many as possible should go, and they should be the best and most distinguished the city can offer. They will make the city famous at these sacred gatherings during peacetime, winning a reputation that mirrors and complements the glory won in war. Upon their return, they shall teach the younger generation that other states' institutions are inferior to their own.

A second category of travelers may also be sent, with the guardians' approval: citizens who wish to observe other societies at greater leisure. No law shall prevent them from going. A city that has no experience of good and bad people, and no contact with the outside world, can never become fully civilized. Citizens cannot properly observe laws through habit alone, without genuinely understanding them.

There are always a few extraordinary individuals in the world whose acquaintance is priceless -- and they emerge as readily in poorly governed cities as in well-governed ones. Citizens of a well-ordered state should constantly seek out such people, traveling by sea and land to find those of incorruptible character, so that they may strengthen institutions that are already sound and repair those that are deficient. Without this ongoing investigation, a city can never remain perfect -- and a poorly conducted investigation is just as harmful as none at all.

Cleinias asked: "How can we conduct such an investigation, and do it well?"
elegant:
The travel law: no one under forty may go abroad, and only in a public capacity -- as diplomat, herald, or sacred delegate. Military service is a separate matter.

Citizens should be sent to the great pan-Hellenic festivals to win peacetime renown paralleling wartime glory, and to return teaching the young that other states' institutions are inferior. A second class of travelers -- approved by the guardians -- should observe other societies freely, because no city can be fully civilized without outside contact, and laws cannot be properly followed through habit alone without understanding.

Most importantly, every era produces a few extraordinary minds in both well-ordered and disordered cities. A good state must constantly seek them out across sea and land, finding incorruptible people who can strengthen what works and repair what is deficient. Without this ongoing inquiry, no city remains perfect.

Cleinias asked how such an examination could be conducted well.
summary: Foreign travel is restricted to citizens over forty acting in public capacities, with exceptions for sacred festival delegations and approved observers. The most important form of travel is seeking out rare, incorruptible individuals in other states whose wisdom can strengthen the city's institutions. Without this ongoing inquiry, no state can remain perfect.
