This metaphor prepares the argument that the Nocturnal Council will serve as the state's Atropos, preserving its laws through philosophical understanding rather than mere enforcement.

narrative:
Cleinias: "It will be no small matter if we can only discover how such a nature can be implanted in anything."

The Athenian Stranger: "But it certainly can be; so much I clearly see."

Cleinias: "Then let us not think of desisting until we have imparted this quality to our laws; for it is ridiculous, after a great deal of labour has been spent, to place a thing at last on an insecure foundation."

Megillus: "I approve of your suggestion, and am quite of the same mind with you."

Cleinias: "Very good. And now what, according to you, is to be the salvation of our government and of our laws, and how is it to be effected?"
refashioned:
Cleinias observed: "It would be no small achievement if we could discover how to implant such permanence in anything."

The Athenian Stranger replied: "But it certainly can be done -- that much I see clearly."

Cleinias urged: "Then let us not stop until we have imparted this quality to our laws. It would be absurd, after all this labor, to place our creation on an insecure foundation."

Megillus agreed: "I approve entirely. I am of the same mind."

Cleinias pressed: "Very good. Then what, in your view, will be the salvation of our government and our laws, and how can it be achieved?"
elegant: Cleinias: discovering how to implant permanence would be a great achievement. The Stranger: it can certainly be done. Cleinias: then we must not stop until we succeed -- it would be absurd to build so much on an insecure foundation. Megillus agrees. Cleinias asks: what will save our government, and how?
summary: The three interlocutors agree that they must not stop until they discover how to give their laws the quality of permanence. After all the labor of constructing the legal code, it would be absurd to leave it on an insecure foundation. Cleinias asks what will provide salvation for their government.
distilled: After building an entire legal code, the three agree it would be absurd to leave it without a mechanism for permanent preservation.
notes: Cleinias, the Athenian Stranger, and Megillus. This brief exchange is dramatically significant -- it is one of the rare moments when all three interlocutors actively participate, and it builds urgency for the Nocturnal Council proposal. Cleinias' architectural metaphor ('insecure foundation') echoes Republic 497c-d on the philosopher-king as the foundation of the just city. Megillus' agreement, though brief, is important because as a Spartan he represents practical political experience endorsing the need for institutional permanence. The question 'how is it to be effected?' drives the remainder of Book 12.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Were we not saying that there must be in our city a council which was to be of this sort: The ten oldest guardians of the law, and all those who have obtained prizes of virtue, were to meet in the same assembly, and the council was also to include those who had visited foreign countries in the hope of hearing something that might be of use in the preservation of the laws, and who, having come safely home, and having been tested in these same matters, had proved themselves to be worthy to take part in the assembly; each of the members was to select some young man of not less than thirty years of age, he himself judging in the first instance whether the young man was worthy by nature and education, and then suggesting him to the others, and if he seemed to them also to be worthy they were to adopt him; but if not, the decision at which they arrived was to be kept a secret from the citizens at large, and more especially from the rejected candidate."

"The meeting of the council was to be held early in the morning, when everybody was most at leisure from all other business, whether public or private -- was not something of this sort said by us before?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Did we not say that our city must have a council of this character? The ten eldest guardians of the law and all those who have won prizes for virtue were to assemble together. The council was also to include those citizens who had traveled abroad seeking knowledge useful for preserving the laws, and who, upon returning safely and being tested in these matters, proved themselves worthy of membership.

Each member was to select a younger companion of at least thirty years of age, judging first by his own assessment whether the young man was worthy by nature and education, and then presenting him to the others. If they too judged him worthy, he was accepted. If not, the rejection was to be kept secret from the general public and especially from the rejected candidate himself.

The council was to meet early in the morning, when everyone is most free from public and private business. Did we not establish something along these lines earlier?
elegant: The council's composition was already outlined: the ten eldest law-guardians, all virtue-prize winners, and tested citizen-observers who traveled abroad for useful knowledge. Each member selects a younger companion (at least thirty), first judging his worth personally, then presenting him to the group. Rejections are kept secret -- especially from the rejected candidate. The council meets at dawn, when all are free from other obligations.
summary: The Athenian Stranger recapitulates the council's already-established composition: the eldest law-guardians, virtue-prize holders, and tested foreign observers, each sponsoring a younger companion. The group meets at dawn and keeps its rejection decisions secret, particularly from rejected candidates.
distilled: The council that will preserve the laws combines the city's most virtuous elders with tested younger members, meeting at dawn in a body whose internal deliberations remain confidential.
notes: The Athenian Stranger recapitulates the Nocturnal Council's structure from earlier discussion (12.20). The secrecy around rejections serves a dual purpose: protecting rejected candidates from public shame and maintaining the council's mystique. The composition -- combining proven virtue, foreign experience, and youth -- mirrors the Academy's own structure of experienced philosophers mentoring younger students. This is Plato's mature institutional solution to the Republic's philosopher-king problem: not a single wise ruler, but a self-perpetuating body of the wisest citizens. Compare Republic 502c-d on the difficulty of establishing philosopher-rulers, and Statesman 292d-293a on the rarity of true political knowledge.

narrative:
Cleinias: "True."

The Athenian Stranger: "Then, returning to the council, I would say further, that if we let it down to be the anchor of the state, our city, having everything which is suitable to her, will preserve all that we wish to preserve."

Cleinias: "What do you mean?"

The Athenian Stranger: "Now is the time for me to speak the truth in all earnestness."

Cleinias: "Well said, and I hope that you will fulfil your intention."

The Athenian Stranger: "Know, Cleinias, that everything, in all that it does, has a natural saviour, as of an animal the soul and the head are the chief saviours."

Cleinias: "Once more, what do you mean?"

The Athenian Stranger: "The well-being of those two is obviously the preservation of every living thing."

Cleinias: "How is that?"

The Athenian Stranger: "The soul, besides other things, contains mind, and the head, besides other things, contains sight and hearing; and the mind, mingling with the noblest of the senses, and becoming one with them, may be truly called the salvation of all."

Cleinias: "Yes, quite so."

The Athenian Stranger: "Yes, indeed; but with what is that intellect concerned which, mingling with the senses, is the salvation of ships in storms as well as in fair weather? In a ship, when the pilot and the sailors unite their perceptions with the piloting mind, do they not save both themselves and their craft?"

Cleinias: "Very true."

The Athenian Stranger: "We do not want many illustrations about such matters. What aim would the general of an army, or what aim would a physician propose to himself, if he were seeking to attain salvation?"

Cleinias: "Very good."

The Athenian Stranger: "Does not the general aim at victory and superiority in war, and do not the physician and his assistants aim at producing health in the body?"

Cleinias: "Certainly."

The Athenian Stranger: "And a physician who is ignorant about the body, that is to say, who knows not that which we just now called health, or a general who knows not victory, or any others who are ignorant of the particulars of the arts which we mentioned, cannot be said to have understanding about any of these matters."

Cleinias: "They cannot."

The Athenian Stranger: "And what would you say of the state? If a person proves to be ignorant of the aim to which the statesman should look, ought he, in the first place, to be called a ruler at all; further, will he ever be able to preserve that of which he does not even know the aim?"

Cleinias: "Impossible."
refashioned:
Cleinias confirmed this was correct.

The Athenian Stranger continued: "Returning to the council -- if we establish it as the anchor of the state, then our city, possessing everything suitable to it, will preserve all that we wish to preserve."

Cleinias asked what he meant.

The Athenian Stranger replied: "Now I must speak the truth with full seriousness. Know this, Cleinias: everything that exists has a natural preserver. In an animal, the soul and the head are the chief preservers."

Cleinias asked him to explain further.

The Athenian Stranger elaborated: "The well-being of those two -- soul and head -- is obviously what preserves every living thing. The soul contains mind, among other things, and the head contains sight and hearing, among other things. When mind mingles with the noblest senses and becomes one with them, this union may truly be called the salvation of all."

Cleinias agreed.

The Athenian Stranger pressed on: "Consider what kind of intellect this is -- the kind that, when united with the senses, saves ships in both storms and fair weather. In a ship, when the pilot and sailors unite their perceptions with the pilot's guiding intelligence, do they not save both themselves and their vessel?"

Cleinias agreed this was true.

The Athenian Stranger continued: "We need not multiply examples. Simply consider: what aim does a general pursue for the army's preservation? What aim does a physician pursue? The general aims at victory and military superiority. The physician and his assistants aim at producing health in the body. A physician ignorant of health, a general ignorant of victory, or anyone ignorant of the specific aim of his art -- none of these can be said to understand their field."

Cleinias confirmed this.

The Athenian Stranger concluded: "And what of the state? If someone is ignorant of the aim toward which the statesman should direct everything, should he even be called a ruler? And will he ever be able to preserve something whose very purpose he does not understand?"

Cleinias replied: "Impossible."
elegant:
The council must serve as the state's anchor. Every living thing has a natural preserver: in an animal, the soul and head. The soul contains mind, the head contains sight and hearing. Mind united with the noblest senses produces salvation.

Examples: a pilot's intelligence united with sailors' perceptions saves the ship. A general aims at victory, a physician at health. Without knowing their aim, none can succeed. The same applies to the state: a ruler ignorant of the statesman's true aim cannot preserve what he does not understand.
summary: Through a series of analogies -- the soul and head preserving the body, the pilot saving the ship, the general pursuing victory, the physician pursuing health -- the Athenian Stranger establishes that every domain requires knowledge of its essential aim. The state is no different: a ruler who does not know the statesman's true aim cannot preserve the state.
distilled: Just as a physician must know health and a general must know victory, a statesman must know the true aim of governance, or he cannot preserve the state.
notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. This passage employs Plato's characteristic craft analogy (techne analogy) -- comparing statesmanship to medicine, navigation, and generalship -- which runs throughout the dialogues from early works like the Gorgias (464b-465e) to the Statesman (293a-e). The crucial move is asking what the statesman's aim is, paralleling the Republic's argument that rulers must know the Form of the Good (Republic 504e-505a). The metaphor of the council as the state's 'anchor' is nautical, connecting to the ship of state imagery used throughout Greek political philosophy. The soul-head analogy will be developed further as the Nocturnal Council becomes the state's 'head' containing its 'mind.'

narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "And therefore, if our settlement of the country is to be perfect, we ought to have some institution, which, as I was saying, will tell what is the aim of the state, and will inform us how we are to attain this, and what law or what man will advise us to that end. Any state which has no such institution is likely to be devoid of mind and sense, and in all her actions will proceed by mere chance."
refashioned: The Athenian Stranger continued: Therefore, if our settlement is to be truly complete, we need an institution that does three things: it identifies the aim of the state, it explains how to achieve that aim, and it determines what laws or what persons can guide us toward it. Any state that lacks such an institution is essentially mindless and senseless, and everything it does will be governed by mere chance.
elegant: A complete state needs an institution that identifies its aim, explains how to achieve it, and determines what laws and leaders can guide it there. Without this, the state is mindless -- all its actions governed by chance.
summary: Every state needs a central institution that can articulate the state's purpose, explain how to achieve it, and identify the laws and leaders needed. Without such an institution, the state acts mindlessly and by chance.
distilled: A state without an institution that knows and articulates its purpose is mindless and acts by mere chance.
notes: The Athenian Stranger. This passage states the core argument for the Nocturnal Council with crystalline clarity. The three functions -- identifying the aim, explaining the method, and selecting the guides -- correspond to theoretical, practical, and evaluative wisdom. The description of a state without such guidance as 'devoid of mind and sense' makes explicit the body-politic analogy: the Nocturnal Council is literally the state's brain. Compare Republic 428b-429a, where the wisdom of the whole city resides in its smallest part (the guardians). The phrase 'proceed by mere chance' (tuche) opposes the Platonic ideal that governance should be guided by knowledge (episteme), not fortune.

narrative:
Cleinias: "Very true."

The Athenian Stranger: "In which, then, of the parts or institutions of the state is any such guardian power to be found? Can we say?"

Cleinias: "I am not quite certain, Stranger; but I have a suspicion that you are referring to the assembly which you just now said was to meet at night."

The Athenian Stranger: "You understand me perfectly, Cleinias; and we must assume, as the argument implies, that this council possesses all virtue; and the beginning of virtue is not to make mistakes by guessing many things, but to look steadily at one thing, and on this to fix all our aims."

Cleinias: "Quite true."
refashioned:
Cleinias agreed.

The Athenian Stranger asked: "In which part or institution of the state, then, is this guardian power to be found? Can we identify it?"

Cleinias replied: "I am not entirely certain, Stranger, but I suspect you are referring to the assembly you just described as meeting before dawn."

The Athenian Stranger confirmed: "You understand me perfectly, Cleinias. And as our argument requires, we must assume that this council possesses all virtue. The beginning of virtue is not to stumble by guessing at many different aims, but to look steadily at one single thing and direct everything toward it."

Cleinias agreed: "Quite true."
elegant: Which institution will serve as the state's guardian intelligence? Cleinias correctly identifies the pre-dawn assembly. The Stranger confirms: this council must possess all virtue. The foundation of virtue is not guessing at many aims but fixing steadily on one -- and directing everything toward it.
summary: Cleinias correctly identifies the Nocturnal Council as the institution that will serve as the state's guardian intelligence. The Athenian Stranger confirms this and states its essential principle: virtue begins with looking steadily at one thing rather than guessing at many, and directing all aims toward it.
distilled: The Nocturnal Council is the state's guardian intelligence, and its virtue lies in fixing steadily on a single aim rather than scattering among many.
notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. Cleinias' identification of the 'assembly which meets at night' gives the Nocturnal Council (nukterinos sullogos) its name -- though it actually meets before dawn. The principle that virtue means 'looking steadily at one thing' rather than 'guessing many things' is the practical application of the Republic's Form of the Good (505a-509b): the philosopher-ruler's power derives from seeing the single unifying principle behind all particular goods. The contrast between 'guessing' (stochazesthai) and 'looking steadily' (blepein) distinguishes empirical groping from genuine knowledge. This is Plato's last word on the unity of virtue -- a question that occupied him from the Protagoras through the Republic.

narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "Then now we shall see why there is nothing wonderful in states going astray -- the reason is that their legislators have such different aims; nor is there anything wonderful in some laying down as their rule of justice, that certain individuals should bear rule in the state, whether they be good or bad, and others that the citizens should be rich, not caring whether they are the slaves of other men or not. The tendency of others, again, is towards freedom; and some legislate with a view to two things at once -- they want to be at the same time free and the lords of other states; but the wisest men, as they deem themselves to be, look to all these and similar aims, and there is no one of them which they exclusively honour, and to which they would have all things look."
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Now we can see why it is no surprise that states go astray -- the reason is that their legislators pursue such different aims. It is equally unsurprising that some establish as their principle of justice that certain individuals should rule, regardless of whether they are good or bad. Others make wealth their aim, indifferent to whether citizens are enslaved to other nations or not. Still others aim primarily at freedom. Some try to pursue two goals simultaneously -- wanting their citizens to be both free and rulers over other states.

And those who consider themselves the wisest of all look to all these aims and more, without settling exclusively on any single one to which everything else should be directed.
elegant: States go astray because their legislators pursue conflicting aims: some prioritize who rules (regardless of merit), others prioritize wealth (regardless of servitude), others freedom, and some want both freedom and empire simultaneously. The supposedly wisest look to all these aims at once, without fixing exclusively on any single one.
summary: States fail because their legislators lack a unified aim. Some prioritize political power, others wealth, others freedom, and some try to combine freedom with imperial domination. Even those considered wisest scatter their attention across multiple aims without settling on one.
distilled: States go astray because their legislators scatter their aims across power, wealth, freedom, and empire, rather than fixing on the single aim of virtue.
notes: The Athenian Stranger. This passage provides a typology of failed political aims that maps onto the degenerate regimes in Republic Book 8: timocracy (rule by the strong), oligarchy (wealth), democracy (freedom), and tyranny (power). The critique of those who pursue 'two things at once' -- freedom and empire -- pointedly describes Athens and perhaps Sparta. The key philosophical move is arguing that even eclecticism (pursuing all these aims without hierarchy) is a failure, because the diversity of aims guarantees internal contradiction. Only virtue as a unified aim can provide coherent direction. Compare Republic 544c-545b on the progression of degenerate constitutions.

narrative:
Cleinias: "Then, Stranger, our former assertion will hold, for we were saying that laws generally should look to one thing only; and this, as we admitted, was rightly said to be virtue."

The Athenian Stranger: "Yes."

Cleinias: "And we said that virtue was of four kinds?"

The Athenian Stranger: "Quite true."

Cleinias: "And that mind was the leader of the four, and that to her the three other virtues and all other things ought to have regard?"
refashioned:
Cleinias said: "Then our earlier position stands, Stranger. We were saying that all laws should be directed toward one thing only, and we correctly identified that one thing as virtue."

The Athenian Stranger confirmed: "Yes."

Cleinias continued: "And we said that virtue has four kinds?"

The Athenian Stranger agreed: "Quite true."

Cleinias concluded: "And that mind is the leader of the four, and that the three other virtues -- along with everything else -- should be directed toward it?"
elegant: Cleinias recapitulates: laws should aim at one thing -- virtue. Virtue has four kinds. Mind leads the four, and the other three virtues along with everything else should be directed toward it.
summary: Cleinias confirms the established position: all laws should aim at virtue, virtue has four kinds (courage, temperance, justice, and wisdom), and mind or wisdom leads the other three. All other things should be subordinated to it.
distilled: All laws should aim at virtue, and among virtue's four kinds, mind is the leader to which the other three must defer.
notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. This recapitulation references the foundational argument of Laws Book 1 (631b-632d), where the four virtues are established with wisdom (phronesis/nous) as their leader. The four cardinal virtues -- wisdom, courage, temperance, justice -- were a standard framework in Platonic thought, developed fully in Republic Book 4 (427e-435b). The hierarchical ordering with mind at the top is crucial for what follows: the Nocturnal Council, as the state's embodiment of mind, must therefore lead all other institutions. Cleinias' active recollection of these principles shows the dialogue's pedagogical progress -- the student can now reconstruct the argument himself.

narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "You follow me capitally, Cleinias, and I would ask you to follow me to the end, for we have already said that the mind of the pilot, the mind of the physician and of the general look to that one thing to which they ought to look; and now we may turn to mind political, of which, as of a human creature, we will ask a question: O wonderful being, and to what are you looking? The physician is able to tell his single aim in life, but you, the superior, as you declare yourself to be, of all intelligent beings, when you are asked are not able to tell. Can you, Megillus, and you, Cleinias, say distinctly what is the aim of mind political, in return for the many explanations of things which I have given you?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: "You follow me admirably, Cleinias, and I ask you to stay with me to the end. We have established that the pilot's mind, the physician's mind, and the general's mind each look toward the one thing they ought to look toward. Now let us turn to the political mind and address it directly, as we would a human being:

'O remarkable being -- toward what are you looking? The physician can state his single aim clearly. But you -- who declare yourself superior to all other intelligent beings -- when asked, you cannot state yours.'

Can you, Megillus, and you, Cleinias, after all the explanations I have provided, tell me distinctly: what is the aim of political intelligence?"
elegant:
The pilot's mind aims at safe passage, the physician's at health, the general's at victory. Now address the political mind directly: 'What are you looking toward? The physician can state his aim clearly -- but you, who claim superiority over all intelligent beings, cannot.'

Can Megillus and Cleinias, after all these explanations, state the aim of political intelligence?
summary: After establishing that each profession's mind has a clear aim, the Athenian Stranger directly challenges the political mind to state its own. He asks Megillus and Cleinias whether, after extensive discussion, they can identify the specific aim of political intelligence.
distilled: The physician knows he aims at health, the general at victory -- but what is the single aim of political intelligence? Most statesmen cannot say.
notes: The Athenian Stranger. The dramatic device of addressing the 'political mind' as though it were a person (prosopopoeia) creates a powerful rhetorical effect -- political wisdom is personified and then found wanting. The question 'to what are you looking?' demands the kind of clear, single-aim answer that Republic 505a-b poses about the Good: what is it that makes everything else worthwhile? The comparison to medicine, navigation, and generalship is a version of the craft analogy that runs from Plato's earliest dialogues (Ion, Hippias Minor) through the Statesman. The challenge to both Cleinias and Megillus personalizes the question -- these practical statesmen represent Cretan and Spartan political wisdom, which Plato respects but finds ultimately inadequate.

narrative:
Cleinias: "We cannot, Stranger."

The Athenian Stranger: "Well, but ought we not to desire to see it, and to see where it is to be found?"

Cleinias: "For example, where?"

The Athenian Stranger: "For example, we were saying that there are four kinds of virtue, and as there are four of them, each of them must be one."

Cleinias: "Certainly."

The Athenian Stranger: "And further, all four of them we call one; for we say that courage is virtue, and that prudence is virtue, and the same of the two others, as if they were in reality not many but one, that is, virtue."

Cleinias: "Quite so."

The Athenian Stranger: "There is no difficulty in seeing in what way the two differ from one another, and have received two names, and so of the rest. But there is more difficulty in explaining why we call these two and the rest of them by the single name of virtue."

Cleinias: "How do you mean?"

The Athenian Stranger: "I have no difficulty in explaining what I mean. Let us distribute the subject between questions and answers."

Cleinias: "Once more, what do you mean?"
refashioned:
Cleinias admitted: "We cannot, Stranger."

The Athenian Stranger replied: "But should we not desire to see it and to discover where it can be found?"

Cleinias asked: "Where, for example?"

The Athenian Stranger began: "We said there are four kinds of virtue, and since there are four, each must be distinct -- each one."

Cleinias agreed.

The Athenian Stranger continued: "Yet we also call all four by a single name. We say courage is virtue, prudence is virtue, and the same for the other two, as though they were not many things but one -- virtue."

Cleinias confirmed this.

The Athenian Stranger elaborated: "It is easy enough to see how any two of them differ from each other and have received different names. But it is far more difficult to explain why we call them all by the single name of virtue."

Cleinias asked what he meant.

The Athenian Stranger proposed: "What I mean is not hard to explain. Let us proceed through questions and answers."
elegant: Cleinias admits they cannot identify the political aim. The Stranger presses: we should desire to see it. The path leads through the problem of virtue's unity. There are four virtues, each distinct -- yet we call them all 'virtue,' as though they were one. It is easy to explain how they differ; it is far harder to explain what makes them all deserve the same name.
summary: Unable to state the aim of political intelligence, the interlocutors approach the question through the problem of virtue's unity and plurality. Each of the four virtues is distinct and has its own name, yet all are called by the single name 'virtue.' Explaining this unity is the harder philosophical task.
distilled: The key to political wisdom lies in understanding what makes the four distinct virtues genuinely one -- the problem of the unity of virtue.
notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The problem of the unity of virtue is one of Plato's most persistent philosophical concerns, addressed in the Protagoras (329c-333b), Meno (73e-74a), Laches (190d-199e), and Republic Book 4. Here, near the end of his last dialogue, Plato returns to it with renewed urgency: the Nocturnal Council must understand not just each virtue individually but what makes them all 'one.' This is equivalent to understanding the Form of the Good in the Republic -- the single principle that unifies all values. The dialectical method of questions and answers recalls the Socratic method at its purest.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Ask me what is that one thing which we call virtue, and then again speak of as two, one part being courage and the other wisdom. I will tell you how that occurs: One of them has to do with fear; in this the beasts also participate, and quite young children -- I mean courage; for a courageous temper is a gift of nature and not of reason. But without reason there never has been, or is, or will be a wise and understanding soul; it is of a different nature."

Cleinias: "That is true."
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger proposed: "Ask me what that one thing is which we call virtue and yet also divide into two, with courage as one part and wisdom as the other. Here is how the division occurs: one of them -- courage -- has to do with fear. Even animals and very young children share in it, because a courageous temperament is a gift of nature, not of reason. But wisdom is entirely different: without reason, there has never been, is not now, and never will be a wise and understanding soul. Wisdom is of a fundamentally different nature from courage."

Cleinias agreed: "That is true."
elegant: The division: courage deals with fear and is shared by animals and children -- it is a gift of nature, not reason. Wisdom requires reason absolutely -- no soul has ever been or will ever be wise without it. They are fundamentally different in nature, yet both are called virtue.
summary: Courage and wisdom differ fundamentally in nature. Courage relates to fear and is a natural endowment shared by animals and children. Wisdom requires reason and has never existed without it. Yet both are called virtue, which raises the question of what unites them.
distilled: Courage is a natural gift even animals possess; wisdom requires reason absolutely -- yet both are called virtue, demanding an explanation of their unity.
notes: The Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The distinction between natural courage (shared with animals) and rational wisdom anticipates Aristotle's distinction between natural virtue and virtue proper (Nicomachean Ethics 1144b). The claim that wisdom is 'of a different nature' from courage directly engages the problem raised in the Protagoras: is all virtue really knowledge? Here Plato acknowledges that courage has a non-rational component, which is more nuanced than the Socratic identification of virtue with knowledge (Protagoras 360d). The phrase 'never has been, is, or will be' gives wisdom a quasi-eternal status -- it cannot arise from nature alone but requires the cultivation of reason. This has direct implications for the Nocturnal Council's educational program.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "I have now told you in what way the two are different, and do you in return tell me in what way they are one and the same. Suppose that I ask you in what way the four are one, and when you have answered me, you will have a right to ask of me in return in what way they are four; and then let us proceed to enquire whether in the case of things which have a name and also a definition to them, true knowledge consists in knowing the name only and not the definition. Can he who is good for anything be ignorant of all this without discredit where great and glorious truths are concerned?"

Cleinias: "I suppose not."

The Athenian Stranger: "And is there anything greater to the legislator and the guardian of the law, and to him who thinks that he excels all other men in virtue, and has won the palm of excellence, than these very qualities of which we are now speaking -- courage, temperance, wisdom, justice?"

Cleinias: "How can there be anything greater?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: "I have explained how courage and wisdom differ. Now you must tell me how they are one and the same. And suppose I then ask how the four virtues are one -- once you answer that, you may ask me in return how the one is four.

But then let us consider this further question: for things that have both a name and a definition, does true knowledge consist in knowing only the name, without the definition? Can anyone of real merit be ignorant of such things without disgrace, especially when great and noble truths are at stake?"

Cleinias replied: "I suppose not."

The Athenian Stranger pressed: "And is there anything of greater importance to the legislator, the guardian of the law, and the person who believes he excels all others in virtue and has won the highest distinction -- anything greater than these very qualities we are discussing: courage, temperance, wisdom, and justice?"

Cleinias answered: "How could there be anything greater?"
elegant:
The two virtues differ -- now explain how they are one. Then how the four are one, and how the one is four. True knowledge requires not just knowing the name of something but also its definition. Can anyone of real worth be ignorant of these things without disgrace?

For the legislator and law-guardian -- the person who claims to excel all others in virtue -- nothing is more important than understanding courage, temperance, wisdom, and justice.
summary: Understanding the unity and diversity of virtue requires knowing not just the names but the definitions. True knowledge grasps both how the four virtues differ and how they are one. For legislators and guardians of the law, nothing is more important than this understanding.
distilled: True knowledge of virtue requires grasping not just its name but its definition -- understanding both how the four virtues differ and what makes them one.
notes: The Athenian Stranger. The distinction between knowing a name and knowing a definition echoes the Seventh Letter (342a-343c), where Plato distinguishes levels of knowledge: name, definition, image, knowledge, and the thing itself. The insistence that guardians must know virtue's definition, not merely its name, establishes the philosophical curriculum for the Nocturnal Council. This is the practical application of the Republic's demand that rulers know the Form of the Good (504e-505b). The reciprocal questioning structure -- 'how are the four one? how is the one four?' -- is classic dialectical method, requiring understanding from both directions. Compare Meno 71b on the impossibility of knowing a thing's properties without knowing what it is.

narrative: The Athenian Stranger: "And ought not the interpreters, the teachers, the lawgivers, the guardians of the other citizens, to excel the rest of mankind, and perfectly to show him who desires to learn and know or whose evil actions require to be punished and reproved, what is the nature of virtue and vice? Or shall some poet who has found his way into the city, or some chance person who pretends to be an instructor of youth, show himself to be better than him who has won the prize for every virtue? And can we wonder that when the guardians are not adequate in speech or action, and have no adequate knowledge of virtue, the city being unguarded should experience the common fate of cities in our day?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: Should not the interpreters of the law, the teachers, the lawgivers, and the guardians of the citizenry surpass all other people? Should they not be able to demonstrate perfectly -- to anyone who wishes to learn, and to anyone whose wrongdoing requires punishment and correction -- the true nature of virtue and vice?

Or shall some poet who wanders into the city, or some random individual who claims to be an instructor of youth, prove himself superior to the person who has won the prize for every form of virtue?

And when the guardians prove inadequate in both speech and action, lacking adequate knowledge of virtue -- when the city is effectively unguarded -- can we be surprised that it suffers the same fate as every other city in our time?
elegant:
The state's interpreters, teachers, lawgivers, and guardians must surpass everyone in demonstrating the nature of virtue and vice -- to those who wish to learn and to those who need correction. Otherwise, some wandering poet or self-proclaimed youth instructor will outshine the supposedly virtuous.

When guardians lack adequate knowledge of virtue in both speech and action, the city is effectively unguarded -- and its collapse is no surprise.
summary: The state's guardians must have superior knowledge of virtue and vice, able to teach the willing and correct the wayward. If they cannot, random poets and self-proclaimed teachers will fill the void. When guardians lack adequate virtue in speech and action, the city is unguarded and inevitably falls.
distilled: When a city's guardians cannot demonstrate virtue in speech and action, the city is effectively unguarded and will inevitably fall.
notes: The Athenian Stranger. The contempt for poets and 'chance persons who pretend to be instructors' echoes the Republic's expulsion of poets (595a-607b) and the Protagoras' critique of the sophists. The argument that guardians must excel in both speech and action -- logos and ergon -- reflects the Platonic insistence that theoretical knowledge must be united with practical virtue, a theme central to the Statesman (258e-260b). The phrase 'the common fate of cities in our day' is a somber reference to the political instability of fourth-century Greece, with constant coups, civil wars, and external invasions. This pragmatic concern drives Plato's insistence on the Nocturnal Council's philosophical competence.

narrative:
Cleinias: "Wonder! No."

The Athenian Stranger: "Well, then, must we do as we said? Or can we give our guardians a more precise knowledge of virtue in speech and action than the many have? Or is there any way in which our city can be made to resemble the head and senses of rational beings because possessing such a guardian power?"

Cleinias: "What, Stranger, is the drift of your comparison?"
refashioned:
Cleinias exclaimed: "No, no surprise at all!"

The Athenian Stranger asked: "Then must we proceed as we proposed? Can we equip our guardians with a more precise knowledge of virtue -- in both speech and action -- than the general public possesses? Is there some way to make our city resemble the head and senses of a rational being, by giving it such a guardian power?"

Cleinias asked: "What exactly is the point of your comparison, Stranger?"
elegant: Can we equip our guardians with knowledge of virtue more precise than what ordinary people have? Can the city be made to resemble the head and senses of a rational being through such guardian power? Cleinias asks what the comparison means.
summary: The Athenian Stranger asks whether the guardians can be given precise knowledge of virtue exceeding ordinary understanding, making the city like a rational being with a functioning head and senses. Cleinias asks him to clarify the analogy.
distilled: The question is whether the city's guardians can possess such precise knowledge of virtue that the city itself becomes like a rational being with a functioning mind.
notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The comparison of the city to a rational being with a 'head and senses' develops the body-politic metaphor introduced at 12.40. The Nocturnal Council is being explicitly identified as the city's head -- its rational faculty. This mirrors the Republic's tripartite soul analogy (435a-441c), where the city's three classes correspond to reason, spirit, and appetite. But the Laws' version is more institutional: instead of a philosophical class, there is a specific deliberative body. The question 'can we give our guardians more precise knowledge than the many have?' is a late echo of the Republic's fundamental question about whether philosopher-rulers can know the Good better than ordinary people.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Do we not see that the city is the trunk, and are not the younger guardians, who are chosen for their natural gifts, placed in the head of the state, having their souls all full of eyes, with which they look about the whole city? They keep watch and hand over their perceptions to the memory, and inform the elders of all that happens in the city; and those whom we compared to the mind, because they have many wise thoughts -- that is to say, the old men -- take counsel and, making use of the younger men as their ministers, and advising with them -- in this way both together truly preserve the whole state."

"Shall this or some other be the order of our state? Are all our citizens to be equal in acquirements, or shall there be special persons among them who have received a more careful training and education?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger explained: Can we not see that the city is like a body's trunk, and the younger guardians -- selected for their natural abilities -- are placed in the head of the state? Their souls are full of eyes with which they survey the entire city. They keep watch, commit their observations to memory, and report everything that happens to the elders. The elders, whom we compared to the mind because of their accumulated wisdom, take counsel using the younger members as their assistants, deliberating alongside them. In this way, both together truly preserve the whole state.

Shall this be the arrangement of our state, or some other? Shall all citizens be equal in their knowledge and training, or shall there be select individuals who receive more rigorous education?
elegant:
The city is the trunk; the younger guardians, with their keen perceptions, are its eyes in the head, surveying everything and reporting to the elders. The elders, as the mind, take counsel and deliberate using the younger members as assistants. Together they preserve the state.

The question: shall all citizens be equal in education, or shall some receive more rigorous training?
summary: The body-politic metaphor is completed: the city is the trunk, younger guardians are the eyes in the head, and the elders are the mind that processes their reports. Together they preserve the state. This raises the critical question of whether all citizens should be educated equally or whether some should receive specialized, more rigorous training.
distilled: The state needs a head -- young guardians as its eyes, elder councilors as its mind -- and this requires that some citizens receive training far beyond what the ordinary citizen receives.
notes: The Athenian Stranger. The organic metaphor of the state is now fully developed: trunk (citizen body), eyes (younger guardians), mind (elder councilors). This tripartite structure maps onto the Republic's three classes (producers/auxiliaries/rulers) but with the important difference that the Laws' system is institutional rather than class-based. The question about educational equality versus specialization is loaded -- Plato will argue for specialization, as he did in Republic 412b-414b (selecting guardians for special education). The image of souls 'full of eyes' recalls Argus Panoptes, the all-seeing mythological guardian, and anticipates the panopticon concept in political theory.

narrative:
Cleinias: "That they should be equal, my good sir, is impossible."

The Athenian Stranger: "Then we ought to proceed to some more exact training than any which has preceded."

Cleinias: "Certainly."

The Athenian Stranger: "And must not that of which we are in need be the one to which we were just now alluding?"

Cleinias: "Very true."

The Athenian Stranger: "Did we not say that the workman or guardian, if he be perfect in every respect, ought not only to be able to see the many aims, but he should press onward to the one? This he should know, and knowing, order all things with a view to it."

Cleinias: "True."

The Athenian Stranger: "And can any one have a more exact way of considering or contemplating anything, than the being able to look at one idea gathered from many different things?"

Cleinias: "Perhaps not."

The Athenian Stranger: "Not 'Perhaps not,' but 'Certainly not,' my good sir, is the right answer. There never has been a truer method than this discovered by any man."

Cleinias: "I bow to your authority, Stranger; let us proceed in the way which you propose."
refashioned:
Cleinias replied: "That all citizens should be equal in training is impossible."

The Athenian Stranger agreed: "Then we must proceed to a more rigorous education than anything we have yet described."

Cleinias confirmed this was necessary.

The Athenian Stranger continued: "And the training we need is the one we just alluded to. We said that a perfect guardian must not only be able to see the many particular aims but must press beyond them to the one. He must know that single principle and order everything with a view to it."

Cleinias agreed.

The Athenian Stranger asked: "And is there any more exact way of examining anything than the ability to see one idea gathered from many different things?"

Cleinias answered hesitantly: "Perhaps not."

The Athenian Stranger corrected him: "Not 'perhaps not' but 'certainly not,' my friend. No truer method has ever been discovered by any human being."

Cleinias yielded: "I defer to your authority, Stranger. Let us proceed as you propose."
elegant:
Equal education for all is impossible. The guardians need more rigorous training: specifically, the ability to see beyond the many particular aims to the one unifying principle, and to order everything accordingly.

The truest method of understanding anything: seeing one idea gathered from many different things. No better method has ever been discovered. The Stranger corrects Cleinias' hesitant 'perhaps not' to an emphatic 'certainly not.'
summary: Since equal education for all citizens is impossible, the guardians must receive more rigorous training. The supreme intellectual method is seeing one unifying idea gathered from many particulars. The Athenian Stranger insists this is not merely the best method but the only true one ever discovered.
distilled: The highest intellectual method -- seeing one idea gathered from many different things -- is the indispensable training for those who would guard the state.
notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The method of 'seeing one idea gathered from many different things' is a description of dialectic -- the supreme philosophical method described in Republic 532a-535a and Phaedrus 265d-266b (collection and division). The Stranger's emphatic correction of Cleinias ('certainly not, not perhaps not') shows that this is non-negotiable: dialectic is the foundation of philosophical training. That 'no truer method has ever been discovered' is one of Plato's strongest endorsements of his own philosophical method. The passage bridges the Republic's theory of Forms (seeing the one behind the many) and the Laws' practical institutional program (the Nocturnal Council's curriculum).

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Then, as would appear, we must compel the guardians of our divine state to perceive, in the first place, what that principle is which is the same in all the four -- the same, as we affirm, in courage and in temperance, and in justice and in prudence, and which, being one, we call as we ought, by the single name of virtue."

"To this, my friends, we will, if you please, hold fast, and not let go until we have sufficiently explained what that is to which we are to look, whether to be regarded as one, or as a whole, or as both, or in whatever way. Are we likely ever to be in a virtuous condition, if we cannot tell whether virtue is many, or four, or one? Certainly, if we take counsel among ourselves, we shall in some way contrive that this principle has a place amongst us; but if you have made up your mind that we should let the matter alone, we will."
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: It appears, then, that we must require the guardians of our divine state to understand, first of all, what principle is the same in all four virtues -- the same element in courage, temperance, justice, and prudence -- which, being one, we rightly designate by the single name of virtue.

This, my friends, we must hold fast and not release until we have adequately explained what it is we should look toward -- whether to regard it as one thing, or as a whole, or as both, or in some other way entirely. Can we ever be in a truly virtuous condition if we cannot say whether virtue is many, or four, or one?

If we take counsel together, we will find some way to ensure this principle has a place among us. But if you have decided we should leave the matter aside, then we will.
elegant:
The guardians must grasp the one principle common to all four virtues -- courage, temperance, justice, prudence -- that justifies calling them all by the single name of virtue. We must hold fast to this inquiry until we explain whether virtue is to be understood as one, as a whole, as both, or in some other way.

Can we ever be truly virtuous if we cannot say whether virtue is many or one? If we deliberate together, we will find a way. But if you prefer to abandon the question, we can.
summary: The guardians must be compelled to understand the single principle that unites the four virtues under one name. Until this question is resolved -- whether virtue is one, many, a whole, or something else -- true virtue remains impossible. The Athenian Stranger frames this as the essential philosophical task.
distilled: The guardians of the state must understand what makes the four virtues one -- without this knowledge, neither they nor the city can be truly virtuous.
notes: The Athenian Stranger. This is the climactic philosophical demand of the Laws: the Nocturnal Council must solve the problem of the unity of virtue. The question 'one, or a whole, or both, or in whatever way' employs the Sophist's method of distinguishing different senses of unity (Sophist 244b-245e). The gentle threat ('if you prefer to abandon the question, we can') is a dialectical device -- Plato knows Cleinias will not abandon it. This passage represents Plato's final attempt at the problem that occupied him from the Protagoras (329c-334c) onward: what is the relationship between the individual virtues and virtue as such? The Republic's answer (the Form of the Good) is here reframed as an institutional curriculum requirement.

narrative:
Cleinias: "We must not, Stranger, by the God of strangers I swear that we must not, for in our opinion you speak most truly; but we should like to know how you will accomplish your purpose."

The Athenian Stranger: "Wait a little before you ask; and let us, first of all, be quite agreed with one another that the purpose has to be accomplished."

Cleinias: "Certainly, it ought to be, if it can be."

The Athenian Stranger: "Well, and about the good and the honourable, are we to take the same view? Are our guardians only to know that each of them is many, or also how and in what way they are one?"

Cleinias: "They must consider also in what sense they are one."

The Athenian Stranger: "And are they to consider only, and to be unable to set forth what they think?"

Cleinias: "Certainly not; that would be the state of a slave."

The Athenian Stranger: "And may not the same be said of all good things -- that the true guardians of the laws ought to know the truth about them, and to be able to interpret them in words, and carry them out in action, judging of what is and what is not well, according to nature?"

Cleinias: "Certainly."
refashioned:
Cleinias exclaimed: "We must not abandon this inquiry, Stranger! By the god of strangers, we must not! In our view, you speak most truly. But we would like to know how you intend to accomplish this."

The Athenian Stranger replied: "Wait before you ask how. First, let us be fully agreed that it must be accomplished."

Cleinias agreed: "It certainly ought to be, if it can be."

The Athenian Stranger extended the principle: "And regarding the good and the honorable -- shall we take the same approach? Shall our guardians know only that each of these concepts is many, or must they also understand how and in what way they are one?"

Cleinias answered: "They must also understand in what sense they are one."

The Athenian Stranger pressed further: "And are they merely to think about this, without being able to articulate their understanding?"

Cleinias replied firmly: "Certainly not. That would be the condition of a slave."

The Athenian Stranger concluded: "And may the same not be said of all good things? The true guardians of the laws must know the truth about them, be able to express that truth in words, and carry it out in action -- judging what is and what is not good, according to nature."

Cleinias confirmed: "Certainly."
elegant:
Cleinias insists they must not abandon the inquiry. The Stranger extends the demand: the guardians must understand not only virtue but also the good and the honorable -- both in their multiplicity and their unity.

Moreover, they must not merely think about these things but articulate their understanding. Inability to express what one knows is 'the condition of a slave.' The true guardian must know the truth about all good things, express it in words, and carry it out in action, judging according to nature.
summary: The guardians must understand the unity of the good and the honorable, not just of virtue. They must also be able to articulate their understanding -- mere silent comprehension is slavish. True guardianship requires knowledge, articulateness, and action, all judging according to nature.
distilled: A guardian who cannot articulate his understanding of the good is no better than a slave; the true guardian must know, speak, and act according to nature.
notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. Cleinias' oath 'by the God of strangers' (Zeus Xenios) is a charming touch -- swearing by the deity of hospitality to insist that the stranger continue his teaching. The distinction between silent understanding and the ability to articulate (logos) is crucial Platonic epistemology: true knowledge must be expressible in rational discourse (compare Theaetetus 201c-210b, where 'true belief with an account' is explored as a definition of knowledge). Cleinias' powerful statement that inarticulate understanding is 'the condition of a slave' equates freedom with the capacity for rational expression -- an idea with profound implications for education and citizenship. The triad of knowing, speaking, and acting recalls the Republic's unity of theory and practice in the philosopher-king.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Is not the knowledge of the Gods which we have set forth with so much zeal one of the noblest sorts of knowledge -- to know that they are, and know how great is their power, as far as in man lies? We do indeed excuse the mass of the citizens, who only follow the voice of the laws, but we refuse to admit as guardians any who do not labour to obtain every possible evidence that there is respecting the Gods; our city is forbidden and not allowed to choose as a guardian of the law, or to place in the select order of virtue, him who is not an inspired man, and has not laboured at these things."

Cleinias: "It is certainly just, as you say, that he who is indolent about such matters or incapable should be rejected, and that things honourable should be put away from him."

The Athenian Stranger: "Are we assured that there are two things which lead men to believe in the Gods, as we have already stated?"

Cleinias: "What are they?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger continued: "Is not the knowledge of the gods -- which we have expounded with such dedication -- among the noblest forms of knowledge? To know that the gods exist and to understand, as far as humanly possible, how great their power is?

We may excuse the general citizenry for simply following the voice of the laws. But we must refuse to accept as guardians anyone who has not worked to obtain every available proof concerning the gods. Our city must be absolutely forbidden from choosing as a guardian of the law, or placing in the select order of virtue, anyone who is not an inspired person and has not devoted serious effort to these matters."

Cleinias agreed: "You are right. Anyone indolent or incapable in such matters should be rejected, and all honors denied to him."

The Athenian Stranger asked: "Do we recall the two things that lead people to belief in the gods, as we stated earlier?"

Cleinias asked: "What are they?"
elegant:
Knowledge of the gods -- that they exist and how great their power is -- ranks among the noblest forms of knowledge. Ordinary citizens may be excused for merely following the laws, but guardians must have labored to obtain every proof concerning the gods. No one uninspired or idle in these matters may serve as a law-guardian or enter the select order of virtue.

Two things lead to belief in the gods, as previously established. Cleinias asks what they are.
summary: Knowledge of the gods' existence and power is among the noblest knowledge. While ordinary citizens may simply follow the laws, guardians must actively pursue theological understanding. No one who has not labored earnestly at this may serve in the highest offices. The Athenian Stranger recalls that two arguments lead to belief in the gods.
distilled: Ordinary citizens may follow the laws by habit, but guardians must possess genuine theological knowledge -- no one uninspired in divine matters may hold the highest offices.
notes: The Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This passage establishes a two-tier epistemological standard: ordinary citizens follow law by habit (doxa/ethos), while guardians must possess genuine knowledge (episteme) of the divine. This mirrors the Republic's philosopher-king requirement (484b-487a) but grounds it specifically in theology rather than the theory of Forms. The reference to 'two things which lead men to believe in the Gods' points back to Laws Book 10's theology (886a-899d), connecting the Nocturnal Council's curriculum to the dialogue's earlier philosophical theology. The term 'inspired' (entheos) is significant -- the council members must not merely study the gods intellectually but be divinely motivated.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "One is the argument about the soul, which has been already mentioned -- that it is the eldest, and most divine of all things, to which motion attaining generation gives perpetual existence; the other was an argument from the order of the motion of the stars, and of all things under the dominion of the mind which ordered the universe. If a man look upon the world not lightly or ignorantly, there was never any one so godless who did not experience an effect opposite to that which the many imagine. For they think that those who handle these matters by the help of astronomy, and the accompanying arts of demonstration, may become godless, because they see, as far as they can see, things happening by necessity, and not by an intelligent will accomplishing good."

Cleinias: "But what is the fact?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger explained: "The first is the argument about the soul -- already discussed -- that it is the oldest and most divine of all things, and that the motion which generates existence grants it perpetual being. The second is the argument from the orderly motion of the stars and of everything governed by the mind that ordered the universe.

If anyone examines the world seriously rather than lightly or ignorantly, there has never been a person so godless that he did not experience the opposite of what most people expect. The common belief is that those who study these matters through astronomy and related sciences become godless, because they appear to see things happening by mechanical necessity rather than by an intelligent will working for the good."

Cleinias asked: "But what is actually the case?"
elegant:
Two arguments lead to belief in the gods: first, the soul's priority -- it is the oldest, most divine thing, granted perpetual existence through motion. Second, the orderly motion of the stars, governed by the mind that ordered the universe.

Contrary to popular belief, serious study of astronomy does not produce godlessness. People assume astronomers see only mechanical necessity -- but the actual effect is the opposite.
summary: Two arguments support belief in the gods: the soul's primacy and immortality, and the orderly motion of the stars revealing cosmic intelligence. Contrary to popular fear, serious astronomical study actually strengthens rather than weakens belief in the divine, because it reveals purposeful order rather than mere mechanical necessity.
distilled: The soul's primacy and the stars' orderly motion are the two great proofs of divine existence, and serious study of astronomy strengthens rather than weakens belief in the gods.
notes: The Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The two arguments for the gods' existence recapitulate Laws Book 10: the cosmological argument from soul (893b-899d) and the argument from celestial order (898d-899b). The defense of astronomy against the charge of promoting atheism is historically significant -- Anaxagoras was prosecuted in Athens for teaching that the sun was a hot stone, and Socrates was accused of studying 'things above' (Apology 18b-c). Plato's reversal -- astronomy properly understood leads to piety, not impiety -- anticipates the medieval astronomical tradition that saw the cosmos as revealing God's design. Compare Timaeus 47a-c on the philosophical value of observing celestial motion.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "Just the opposite, as I said, of the opinion which once prevailed among men, that the sun and stars are without soul. Even in those days men wondered about them, and that which is now ascertained was then conjectured by some who had a more exact knowledge of them -- that if they had been things without soul, and had no mind, they could never have moved with numerical exactness so wonderful; and even at that time some ventured to hazard the conjecture that mind was the orderer of the universe."

"But these same persons again mistaking the nature of the soul, which they conceived to be younger and not older than the body, once more overturned the world, or rather, I should say, themselves; for the bodies which they saw moving in heaven all appeared to be full of stones, and earth, and many other lifeless substances, and to these they assigned the causes of all things. Such studies gave rise to much atheism and perplexity, and the poets took occasion to be abusive -- comparing the philosophers to she-dogs uttering vain howlings, and talking other nonsense of the same sort. But now, as I said, the case is reversed."

Cleinias: "How so?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger explained: "The truth is the exact opposite of what was once commonly believed -- that the sun and stars are soulless objects. Even in those early times, people marveled at the heavenly bodies. And some, with more precise understanding, conjectured what is now established: that if these bodies had no soul or mind, they could never move with such astonishing mathematical precision. Some even dared to conjecture that mind was the ordering principle of the entire universe.

But these same thinkers made a critical error about the soul. They conceived it as younger than, and therefore dependent on, the body -- thereby overturning the truth, or rather, overturning themselves. They looked at the heavenly bodies and saw only stones, earth, and lifeless matter, and they attributed the causes of everything to these material substances. Such studies produced widespread atheism and intellectual confusion, and the poets seized the opportunity to mock the philosophers -- comparing them to dogs howling in vain and heaping similar abuse upon them.

But now, as I said, the situation is entirely reversed."

Cleinias asked: "How so?"
elegant:
The truth reverses the old belief that stars are soulless. Even early thinkers marveled at celestial precision and conjectured that mind orders the universe. But they erred critically: they made soul younger than body, saw only stones and matter in the heavens, and attributed all causes to lifeless substances. This produced atheism, and poets mocked philosophers as 'dogs howling in vain.'

Now the case is reversed.
summary: Early natural philosophers correctly observed the mathematical precision of celestial motion and conjectured that mind orders the universe. But they made the fatal error of subordinating soul to body, seeing only matter in the heavens. This materialism produced atheism and public ridicule of philosophy. Now the correct understanding -- that soul is prior to body -- has reversed the situation.
distilled: Early philosophers correctly saw cosmic order but erred in making soul subordinate to body; correcting this error reverses materialism into genuine theology.
notes: The Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This is a compressed history of pre-Socratic philosophy. The 'exact knowledge' likely refers to Pythagorean mathematical astronomy. The error of making soul 'younger than body' describes the materialist natural philosophers (Anaxagoras, Democritus) who explained everything through physical causes. The 'stones and earth' description of the sun recalls Anaxagoras' specific teaching (Diogenes Laertius 2.8). The poets' mockery of philosophers as 'she-dogs' is probably a reference to Aristophanes' Clouds and similar comic attacks on natural philosophy. The 'reversal' represents Plato's own philosophical intervention: by establishing soul's priority (Laws 10, 891e-896e), he reclaims astronomy for theology. Compare Phaedo 97b-99c on Socrates' disappointment with Anaxagoras.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "No man can be a true worshipper of the Gods who does not know these two principles -- that the soul is the eldest of all things which are born, and is immortal and rules over all bodies; moreover, as I have now said several times, he who has not contemplated the mind of nature which is said to exist in the stars, and gone through the previous training, and seen the connection of music with these things, and harmonized them all with laws and institutions, is not able to give a reason of such things as have a reason."

"And he who is unable to acquire this in addition to the ordinary virtues of a citizen, can hardly be a good ruler of a whole state; but he should be the subordinate of other rulers. Wherefore, Cleinias and Megillus, let us consider whether we may not add to all the other laws which we have discussed this further one -- that the nocturnal assembly of the magistrates, which has also shared in the whole scheme of education proposed by us, shall be a guard set according to law for the salvation of the state. Shall we propose this?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger concluded: "No one can be a true worshipper of the gods without knowing two principles: that the soul is the oldest of all created things, is immortal, and rules over all bodies. Furthermore, as I have stated repeatedly, anyone who has not contemplated the intelligence pervading nature as revealed in the stars, who has not undergone the required preliminary training, who has not perceived the connection between music and these cosmic realities, and who has not harmonized all of this with laws and institutions -- such a person cannot give a rational account of things that have rational explanations.

Anyone who cannot acquire this knowledge in addition to the ordinary virtues of citizenship is unfit to rule an entire state. He should be subordinate to other rulers.

Therefore, Cleinias and Megillus, let us consider whether we should add one more law to all those we have discussed: that the nocturnal assembly of magistrates, which has also participated in the complete educational program we have proposed, shall be established by law as a guard for the salvation of the state. Shall we propose this?"
elegant:
True worship requires two principles: the soul's primacy, immortality, and sovereignty over bodies; and the contemplation of cosmic intelligence in the stars, connected to music, harmonized with laws and institutions. Without this, one cannot give a rational account of rational things.

Anyone lacking this knowledge beyond ordinary civic virtue is unfit to rule -- he should serve under others. Therefore: shall we establish by law that the nocturnal assembly, educated in this full program, shall serve as the state's guardian for its salvation?
summary: True worship and true governance require knowledge of the soul's primacy and of cosmic intelligence as revealed in astronomy, music, and their connection to law. Without this comprehensive philosophical education, one cannot rule. The Athenian Stranger formally proposes establishing the Nocturnal Council as the state's legally constituted guardian.
distilled: The Nocturnal Council -- educated in the soul's primacy, cosmic order, and the harmony of music with law -- shall be the legally established guardian for the state's salvation.
notes: The Athenian Stranger. This is the formal proposal of the Nocturnal Council as the capstone institution. The required curriculum -- theology (soul's primacy), astronomy (cosmic intelligence), harmonics (music), and legislation -- mirrors the Republic's philosopher-king education (Republic 521c-535a: arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, harmonics, dialectic). The phrase 'give a reason of such things as have a reason' (logon didonai) is the hallmark of genuine knowledge in Platonic epistemology (Meno 98a, Theaetetus 201c-d). The integration of music with cosmic and political order reflects the Pythagorean tradition Plato deeply valued. This is Plato's final institutional proposal -- his last word on how philosophy should be embedded in governance.

narrative:
Cleinias: "Certainly, my good friend, we will if the thing is in any degree possible."

The Athenian Stranger: "Let us make a common effort to gain such an object; for I too will gladly share in the attempt. Of these matters I have had much experience, and have often considered them, and I dare say that I shall be able to find others who will also help."

Cleinias: "I agree, Stranger, that we should proceed along the road in which God is guiding us; and how we can proceed rightly has now to be investigated and explained."

The Athenian Stranger: "O Megillus and Cleinias, about these matters we cannot legislate further until the council is constituted; when that is done, then we will determine what authority they shall have of their own; but the explanation of how this is all to be ordered would only be given rightly in a long discourse."

Cleinias: "What do you mean, and what new thing is this?"
refashioned:
Cleinias replied eagerly: "Certainly, my friend, we will do this if it is at all possible."

The Athenian Stranger offered: "Let us make a joint effort toward this goal. I will gladly participate in the attempt. I have considerable experience in these matters and have thought about them extensively, and I believe I can find others who will also contribute."

Cleinias agreed: "We should proceed along the path where God is leading us. How to proceed correctly is what must now be investigated and explained."

The Athenian Stranger cautioned: "Megillus and Cleinias, we cannot legislate further on these matters until the council itself is actually constituted. Once it exists, we can determine what authority it shall hold. But explaining how all of this should be properly ordered would require a very lengthy discourse."

Cleinias asked: "What do you mean? What new matter is this?"
elegant:
Cleinias agrees to establish the council if possible. The Stranger offers to lead the effort from his own experience. Cleinias says they should follow where God leads.

But the Stranger cautions: further legislation on the council's authority is impossible until the council actually exists. Explaining the proper ordering of everything would require a very long discourse. Cleinias asks what he means.
summary: All three agree to establish the Nocturnal Council, with the Athenian Stranger offering his experience to lead the effort. However, he cautions that the council's specific authority cannot be legislated until it is actually constituted, and that a full explanation of its ordering would require extensive further discourse.
distilled: The Nocturnal Council's authority cannot be fully legislated in advance -- it must first be constituted, and its proper ordering requires discourse beyond what legislation alone can provide.
notes: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. The admission that the council's authority cannot be fully predetermined is a profound concession to institutional indeterminacy -- the wisest body must have some latitude to determine its own scope. This distinguishes the Laws from the Republic, where the philosopher-king's authority is more clearly defined. The Stranger's claim of 'much experience' is dramatically interesting -- who is this Athenian with extensive philosophical experience? Many scholars see him as Plato's self-portrait. Cleinias' invocation of divine guidance ('the road in which God is guiding us') frames the entire legislative project as divinely ordained, consistent with the Laws' theological foundations.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "In the first place, a list would have to be made out of those who by their ages and studies and dispositions and habits are well fitted for the duty of a guardian. In the next place, it will not be easy for them to discover themselves what they ought to learn, or become the disciple of one who has already made the discovery. Furthermore, to write down the times at which, and during which, they ought to receive the several kinds of instruction, would be a vain thing; for the learners themselves do not know what is learned to advantage until the knowledge which is the result of learning has found a place in the soul of each."

"And so these details, although they could not be truly said to be secret, might be said to be incapable of being stated beforehand, because when stated they would have no meaning."

Cleinias: "What then are we to do, Stranger, under these circumstances?"
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger explained: "First, a roster must be drawn up of those who -- by age, education, character, and habits -- are qualified for the guardian's duty. But then the difficulty begins: it will not be easy for them to discover on their own what they need to learn, or to find a teacher who has already made that discovery.

Moreover, it would be pointless to prescribe in advance exactly when and for how long each type of instruction should be received. The learners themselves do not know what knowledge will prove truly valuable until that knowledge has actually taken root in each person's soul.

These details are not secret, strictly speaking. But they are incapable of being stated in advance with any meaning, because their significance becomes apparent only through the experience of learning itself."

Cleinias asked: "What are we to do, then, Stranger, given these circumstances?"
elegant:
First, identify those qualified by age, education, character, and habits. But then: the guardians cannot easily discover what they need to learn, nor find the right teacher. Prescribing a curriculum in advance is futile -- learners do not know what knowledge is truly valuable until it has taken root in the soul.

These details are not secret -- they are simply inexpressible in advance, because they gain meaning only through the experience of learning. Cleinias asks what should be done.
summary: Selecting qualified council members is only the first step. The deeper challenge is that the curriculum cannot be meaningfully prescribed in advance -- learners only understand what knowledge is valuable after it has taken root in their souls. These educational truths are not secret but cannot be stated meaningfully before the experience of learning.
distilled: The most important knowledge cannot be prescribed in advance because its value becomes apparent only through the transformative experience of learning itself.
notes: The Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This passage contains one of Plato's most profound epistemological insights: that certain knowledge is 'incapable of being stated beforehand' -- not because it is esoteric or secret, but because understanding requires the prior transformation of the learner. This directly echoes the Seventh Letter (341c-d): 'There is no writing of mine about these things, nor will there ever be. It is not something that can be put into words like other branches of learning.' The impossibility of a fixed curriculum also connects to the Meno's paradox of inquiry (80d-e): you cannot search for what you do not know. This is Plato at his most anti-systematic -- the highest education cannot be systematized.

narrative:
The Athenian Stranger: "As the proverb says, the answer is no secret, but open to all of us: We must risk the whole on the chance of throwing, as they say, thrice six or thrice ace, and I am willing to share with you the danger by stating and explaining to you my views about education and nurture, which is the question coming to the surface again. The danger is not a slight or ordinary one, and I would advise you, Cleinias, in particular, to see to the matter; for if you order rightly the city of the Magnetes, or whatever name God may give it, you will obtain the greatest glory; or at any rate you will be thought the most courageous of men in the estimation of posterity."

"Dear companions, if this our divine assembly can only be established, to them we will hand over the city; none of the present company of legislators, as I may call them, would hesitate about that.
