And the state will be perfected and become a waking reality, which a little while ago we attempted to create as a dream and in idea only, mingling together reason and mind in one image, in the hope that our citizens might be duly mingled and rightly educated; and being educated, and dwelling in the citadel of the land, might become perfect guardians, such as we have never seen in all our previous life, by reason of the saving virtue which is in them."

Megillus: "Dear Cleinias, after all that has been said, either we must detain the Stranger, and by supplications and in all manner of ways make him share in the foundation of the city, or we must give up the undertaking."

Cleinias: "Very true, Megillus; and you must join with me in detaining him."

Megillus: "I will."
refashioned:
The Athenian Stranger declared: "As the proverb says, the answer is no secret -- it is open to all of us. We must stake everything on a single throw, as they say -- risking thrice six or thrice ace. And I am willing to share the danger with you by stating and explaining my views on education and nurture, which is the question that keeps rising to the surface.

The stakes are neither trivial nor ordinary. I would advise you, Cleinias, to attend to this matter with the greatest care. If you establish the city of Magnesia rightly -- or whatever name God may give it -- you will win the greatest glory. At the very least, posterity will consider you the most courageous of men.

Dear companions, if this divine assembly can only be established, we will hand the city over to it. None of us legislators -- as I may call our present company -- would hesitate about that. And then the state will be perfected and become a waking reality. Until now, we have been attempting to create it as a dream and in idea only, mingling reason and mind into a single image, hoping that our citizens might be properly blended and rightly educated. Educated in this way, dwelling in the citadel of the land, they would become perfect guardians -- the like of which we have never seen in all our lives -- by reason of the saving virtue within them."

Megillus urged: "Dear Cleinias, after everything that has been said, either we must detain the Stranger and by every means of persuasion make him participate in the city's foundation, or we must abandon the entire enterprise."

Cleinias replied: "Very true, Megillus. And you must join me in persuading him to stay."

Megillus answered: "I will."
elegant:
The answer is no secret: we must risk everything on a single throw. The Stranger will share the danger by explaining his views on education. The stakes are immense -- Cleinias will win either the greatest glory or at least a reputation for supreme courage.

If the divine assembly can be established, the city is handed over to it without hesitation. The state, until now a dream mingling reason and mind in one image, will become a waking reality. Its citizens, properly educated and dwelling in the citadel, will become perfect guardians possessed of saving virtue -- unlike any the world has seen.

Megillus insists: either they detain the Stranger to help found the city, or the whole project must be abandoned. Cleinias agrees. Megillus commits to persuading him to stay.
summary: The Athenian Stranger commits to sharing his views on education despite the risk, urging Cleinias to establish the city of Magnesia for the greatest glory. If the Nocturnal Council can be founded, the state will transform from a dream into reality, producing perfect guardians through saving virtue. The dialogue ends with Megillus and Cleinias agreeing to detain the Stranger to participate in the city's actual foundation.
distilled: If the Nocturnal Council is established, the dream of a perfectly governed city becomes a waking reality -- and the dialogue ends with an invitation to transform philosophy into action.
notes: The Athenian Stranger, Megillus, and Cleinias. The final passage of Plato's final dialogue. The gambling metaphor ('thrice six or thrice ace' -- the best and worst dice throws) frames the entire legislative project as a wager on reason's power to shape political reality. The image of the state as a 'dream' becoming a 'waking reality' inverts the Republic's Cave allegory (514a-517c), where the philosopher moves from dream-shadows to waking truth -- here, it is the state itself that must awaken. The phrase 'mingling together reason and mind in one image' describes the Nocturnal Council as a fusion of theoretical and practical wisdom. The open ending -- with Megillus and Cleinias urging the Stranger to stay and help -- leaves the actual founding unfinished, as though inviting the reader to continue the work. This is Plato's last word: philosophy must not remain theoretical but must risk itself in political reality.
